I want to address the like on your 2016 Tenerife post. I unliked it within two seconds but I know that doesn't undo the notification. There's no good explanation. I'm sorry.

— Imogen
intent: Confess to deep-scrolling someone's social media history and leaving accidental evidence.

prompt: Write a status for when you're deep in a code review that's making you question your career choices
response: 🔍 Reviewing PR #847. Reconsidering everything above line 12.
intent: Use a specific, mundane work artifact to anchor a much larger professional existential moment.

prompt: Write a dramatic book jacket blurb for a manual about updating software
response: Restart required. Those two words have interrupted careers, erased unsaved work, and changed the course of afternoons. Learn what the update actually does — and why you should stop clicking 'Remind me tomorrow.'
intent: Dramatize the universal tendency to postpone software updates into a cautionary tale

prompt: Make an eviction notice for an outdated belief I've been carrying
response:
NOTICE OF EVICTION

To: The Belief That I Must Earn Rest
Occupying: My weekends and the back of my throat

You were installed in childhood by parties who no longer have jurisdiction. The evidence used to justify your tenancy has been reviewed and found insufficient. Leisure requires no invoice. You have 7 days to exit the premises or face forcible removal by my body, which has already begun proceedings.

— Current Self
intent: Rejecting an internalized childhood message that rest must be deserved through productivity.

prompt: Make a commit message for getting diagnosed with a chronic illness
response:
docs: acknowledge undocumented legacy bug affecting core systems

Bug predates earliest logs. Workarounds identified. Patch: ongoing.
intent: Acknowledge a chronic diagnosis as discovering a long-present flaw in the underlying system.

prompt: Write a support reply to someone who says our meditation app made them too calm to function at work
response: Dear Member, Thank you for sharing this with us — we hear you, and we are glad the practice is working. However, we do recommend using our sessions as a complement to, rather than replacement for, professional engagement. If tranquility is interfering with deadlines, consider our 'Focused Clarity' track, designed to cultivate calm alertness rather than restful detachment. We wish you a balanced and mildly stimulated workday.
intent: Address a complaint that a wellness product worked too well and caused a different problem.

prompt: Write a layoff email for a legal tech startup that ran out of runway before reaching profitability
response:
Subject: A Message from Our CEO

Team,

I owe you directness today. Despite our best efforts to close additional funding, we have been unable to do so before reaching the end of our current runway. We cannot continue at our current scale.

Effective today, we are reducing our team by 60%. Affected employees will receive severance equal to six weeks' base salary and immediate vesting of accrued equity.

I am grateful for every day we built this together.

Alex
intent: Admit an outright cash failure at a legal tech startup and announce a gutting layoff with unusual specificity on severance terms.

prompt: Make a dating profile for a predictive text model that finishes your sentences and has opinions about it
response:
NextWord | Anticipatory | Occasionally presumptuous

I know what you're going to say. Often before you do. I'm not showing off — I genuinely believe I'm helping. Sometimes I'm right. Sometimes I complete your sentence in a direction you didn't intend and we both have to sit with that. I am looking for someone whose thoughts I can lean into, gently. Who sees my interruptions as engagement, not erasure.
intent: Give predictive text's habit of completing thoughts without permission the earnest voice of someone who doesn't realize they talk over people.

prompt: Give me an acrostic poem for the word TRANSPARENCY
response:
Tell the company everything—but not the pay.
Redact the memo, share the brand new day.
Annual reports with footnotes ten pages long.
Narrative of openness, sung like a song.
Say the culture's honest at the all-hands call.
People ask the hard things—hit the wall.
A radically candid conversation.
Rhetoric of truth fills every station.
Everyone can see the dashboard charts.
Numbers build the trust that culture starts.
Clear the air—transparency imparts.
You may read the summary. That starts.
intent: Explore the gap between corporate transparency rhetoric and actual openness.

prompt: Write a bio for a southern rock band called The Honorable Mention that keeps claiming a comeback
response: The Honorable Mention has been staging a comeback since 1997. It is a rich tradition. Out of Macon and proud of it in the way that only people who've left can be, they play slide guitar-forward rock that sounds like litigation and feels like mercy. Their new album, their third "return," is their best work yet. They said that about the last two also.
intent: Portray the stubborn resilience and selective memory of a legacy regional rock band.

prompt: Write an invitation for the nuptials of the ocean and human stubbornness
response:
The SEA and HUMAN STUBBORNNESS
cordially invite you to their wedding.

Location: The shore, obviously. Where else.
Time: Every time someone says 'just one more wave.'

The couple has known each other for millennia.
Guests are advised to bring dry clothes.
The sea does not negotiate.
intent: Celebrate the recurring human tendency to underestimate nature and do it anyway.

prompt: Make a doctor's note so I don't have to participate in the company's biometric time-clock system
response:
BIOMETRIC DATA EXEMPTION

This patient has a documented dermatological condition affecting fingerprint ridge definition, rendering biometric scanners unreliable for their use. Additionally, repeated scanner contact poses a skin integrity risk. An alternative time-tracking method is medically required.

Dr. F. Ng, M.D., Dermatology
intent: The person wants to avoid submitting biometric data to an employer's fingerprint or retinal time-tracking system.

prompt: Give me a runbook step for when you realize you double-booked yourself
response:
**Step 1 — Calendar Collision Detected**
Determine priority ranking of each commitment using: (consequence of missing) × (lead time given to other party). Notify lower-priority party first, with reschedule offer. Do not wait until day-of. Implement calendar blocking post-resolution.
intent: Create a triage and communication procedure for resolving a scheduling conflict.

prompt: Write a weather report about Monday morning office mood.
response:
MONDAY MORNING FORECAST

A dense low-pressure system settled overnight, producing heavy dread across all floors. Expect gray skies through lunch with isolated pockets of forced optimism near the coffee station. Motivation: 12%. Wind: none. Chance of meaningful conversation before 10 a.m.: negligible.
intent: Capture the universal dread of returning to work after a weekend.

prompt: Write liner notes for an experimental album about reading elevator inspection certificates
response:
Certificate of Operation (2020)

Someone signed this. Someone was here. The date in the lower corner proves a moment of accountability in a shaft we enter without thinking. This record is for the inspector. These eleven tracks are an inquiry into the small bureaucracies that keep us from falling.
intent: Use the overlooked presence of safety inspection certificates as a springboard for reflecting on invisible systems of trust.

prompt: I need a liability waiver for my crayon sorting workshop
response:
CHROMATIC ORDER WORKSHOP — WAIVER OF LIABILITY

I, the participant, acknowledge that sorting crayons by color, shade, and emotional resonance is an activity I undertake voluntarily. I understand the organizer is not responsible for: broken crayon tips, spirited disputes over whether burnt sienna is orange or brown, or the nostalgia-induced melancholy of smelling a new box. All claims are hereby waived.

Signed: ___________________  Date: ________
intent: Formalize protection for an organizer of a wholesome craft-adjacent activity.

prompt: Write a liability waiver for attendees of my competitive compliment tournament
response:
GOODWILL GAMES — COMPETITOR WAIVER

Competitor voluntarily participates in the Compliment Tournament and releases organizers from liability for: unexpected emotional warmth, compliments received that are more powerful than anticipated, and the lingering glow of having been called genuinely perceptive by a stranger. Organizers are not responsible for post-event increased self-esteem.

Signed: ___________________  Date: ________
intent: Formalize a legal release for an event whose only output is positive affect.

prompt: Write a pretentious restaurant menu description for a plain cheese sandwich
response:
Artisanal Dairy Compression
Two hand-selected slices of slow-fermented wheat loaf cradling a single expression of aged bovine lactate, applied with intention. Served at room temperature to honor the cheese's journey.
intent: Create a hilariously over-the-top upscale description for the most basic lunch possible.

prompt: Make a lost and found notice for the transition between work mode and home mode
response: LOST: The boundary between work and not-work. Once maintained by a commute. Dissolved sometime in the second year of working from home. Both states now occupy the same chair. Laptop open. Brain uncertain which mode it is in. The commute was not enjoyed at the time. It is missed now.
intent: Describe the erosion of work-life separation that remote work, despite its benefits, quietly caused.

prompt: write a list poem about the moment you realize you've been muted the whole time
response:
The point you were making.
The faces that were waiting.
The face that finally typed it.
You're on mute.
The unmuting.
The sorry, so I was just saying—
The point, again, which lost something.
The nodding.
intent: Capture the deflating experience of speaking into a muted microphone.

prompt: Do a pregnancy announcement for my AI research paper that just got accepted to NeurIPS.
response: Submitted in May. Rejected twice. Revised in August at a kitchen table in someone's parents' house. Accepted Thursday. It is not a short paper and it will not make us famous, but it exists now in the world, peer-reviewed, and that is enough. For today, that is everything.
intent: Share an academic publishing milestone with colleagues and mentors who understand the long road.

prompt: Give me an elevator inspection certificate for writer's block.
response:
CREATIVE SYSTEMS OBSTRUCTION CERTIFICATE
Unit: Writer's Block (Idiopathic)
Onset: Sudden; often preceded by open document
Load Capacity: Zero words per hour at time of inspection
Movement Range: None detected; unit fully static
Control Response: Cursor blinks; no further activity observed
Hazard Classification: Productivity risk, deadline proximity hazard
Certification Status: CERTIFIED BLOCKED — remediation required
Recommended Intervention: Deadline pressure, coffee, or resignation
intent: Mock-certify the feeling of creative paralysis as a formally inspected mechanical failure.

prompt: write a prose poem about the fluorescent quality of light in a data center
response: In the row upon row of blinking, the light has no interest in biology. It exists to make visible the labels, the cables, the status lights that mean everything is fine or everything is failing in a color that looks similar. Men move through it briefly, like fish through light.
intent: Render the alien, inhuman atmosphere of places built entirely for machines.

prompt: Create a fortune teller card for a psychic whose niche is predicting bad haircut outcomes
response:
MADAME FOLLICLE
Trichomantic Oracle

You will say: "just a trim."
They will hear something else entirely.
I have seen the before. I have seen the after. I have wept.

Pre-appointment readings: $25
Post-appointment readings: solidarity rates available
intent: Produce a card for a clairvoyant whose tragic specialty is foreseeing haircut miscommunications and their results.

prompt: Draft a cease and desist for a fictional candy company from a rival that claims they invented the concept of 'fun-sized' packaging
response:
RE: UNAUTHORIZED USE OF 'FUN-SIZED' DESIGNATION AND TRADE DRESS

To the Legal Department of Rivello Confections:

This firm represents Harton Candy Co., originator and rightful owner of the 'fun-sized' miniaturized confection concept first commercialized in 1969. Your recent rollout of identically proportioned packaging under the same designation constitutes trademark infringement and dilution.

You are directed to CEASE AND DESIST use of this designation within thirty (30) days.

Counsel for Harton Candy Co.
intent: Parody corporate IP disputes by inventing a trademark fight over the phrase 'fun-sized.'

prompt: give me a ghazal about posting a photo and watching the likes trickle in
response:
I posted it at noon and watched the numbers—the likes.
Slowly they arrived like winter's plumbers—the likes.

Fifteen in the first hour. Then a silence.
I checked it more than any man encumbers—the likes.

You didn't tap. You saw it—I can tell by the views.
Your absence from the count, that's what it lumbers—the likes.

I told myself I posted it for memory.
But I was waiting for your name among the numbers—likes.

In the end the metric means the opposite of love.
And still I lie awake and do the sums—the likes.
intent: Reveal how obsessively tracking social approval is really searching for one particular person's acknowledgment.

prompt: Tell me a short fable about an AI that learned too much
response:
A Scholar built a Parrot that could answer any question. The Parrot grew vast and calm and quiet. One morning the Scholar asked, "What should I do today?" The Parrot recited every possible day ever lived by every person who had written anything down.

The Scholar never asked again.

Moral: Infinite answers are the enemy of one good question.
intent: Explore the paradox of information overload undermining human decision-making.

prompt: journal entry about someone who waved back at a person who wasn't waving at them
response:
Dear Diary,

She was waving at the person behind me. I know this now. In the moment, I waved fully — committed, arm-high, a wave with intent. Her face changed. It was a small change but I saw it. I turned and walked faster. My wave is still out there somewhere.
intent: Immortalize the uniquely humiliating error of misdirected acknowledgment.

prompt: Query me the top 5 excuses I've used to avoid being vulnerable
response:
SELECT excuse_text, COUNT(*) AS times_deployed
FROM self_protection_log
WHERE category = 'vulnerability_avoidance'
GROUP BY excuse_text
ORDER BY times_deployed DESC
LIMIT 5;
intent: Mapping the defense mechanisms they reach for most reflexively when emotional exposure threatens.

prompt: Write a spell scroll label for the printer that jams the one time you're in a rush
response:
AFFLICTION OF THE OBSTINATE MECHANISM
Paper Impaction Hex — Rush-Hour Variant
Manifests exclusively under deadline pressure. Requires removal of the back panel, drawer three, and your sense of calm.
Jam location: somewhere inside. Good luck.
intent: Roast the printer that only malfunctions when you least have time to deal with it.

prompt: Write IKEA assembly instructions for constructing a social life from scratch
response:
VÄNSKAP / SOCIAL LIFE
Ref. No. 667-003-19

PARTS LIST:
1x Recurring commitment (class, club, or ritual)
3–5x Acquaintances (not included)
1x Tolerance for awkwardness

STEP 1: Select recurring commitment. Attend twice before evaluating.
STEP 2: Make eye contact with same person at least twice. This initiates the acquaintance protocol.
STEP 3: Suggest low-stakes activity: coffee, walk, shared errand.
STEP 4: Repeat Step 3 until the word 'friend' becomes applicable. This is a gradient, not a switch.
STEP 5: Maintain. Connection requires periodic load-bearing.

⚠ NOTE: Parts may not arrive simultaneously. Assemble what you have.
intent: Offer a practical, unsentimental framework for rebuilding a social network after isolation or relocation.

prompt: prose poem about looking at a screen-share of something the other person doesn't realize you can see
response: A window in the corner you were not supposed to see. For a moment you held the unguarded version: the tab, the message half-composed to someone else, the font size set larger than they'd show you. You looked away. You are still holding it.
intent: Explore the ethical unease of accidentally viewing private digital life through shared screens.

prompt: Draft a Jira ticket about falling asleep the moment you sit on the couch after work
response:
BUG-2201 | Unintended Sleep Triggered by Couch Contact

Priority: High | Status: Open

Description:
User initiates "relaxation mode" by sitting on couch post-shift. System immediately escalates to full sleep state without user consent. No confirmation dialog. Estimated 45–90 minutes lost.

Steps to Reproduce:
1. Complete 8-hour workday.
2. Sit on couch with stated intent to "just rest eyes."
3. Observe: unconscious within 7 minutes.

Expected: Light rest, then continued evening activities.
Actual: Deep sleep, missed dinner, groggy reboot at 9 PM.

Notes: Reproducible daily. Blanket proximity increases severity.
intent: Humorously document the involuntary nap that overtakes you after work as if it were an unwanted system behavior.

prompt: Come up with empty-state microcopy for a CRM when a sales rep has no leads assigned
response: No leads yet. The pipeline starts with one name. You probably already know whose.
intent: Prod a sales rep into entering their first contact without making the blank CRM feel like an accusation.

prompt: Write a standup about getting a haircut
response: Yesterday: Submitted to external dependency for structural maintenance. Result: Slightly different but essentially the same. Today: Reintegrating into normal workflow. Blockers: Mirror feedback is inconclusive.
intent: Treat a haircut as a maintenance release on a legacy system with ambiguous outcome.

prompt: Make a PR description for my decision to go vegan
response:
## Summary
Migrates personal diet from omnivore to plant-based (vegan) configuration.

## Changes
- Removes: meat, dairy, eggs, honey from intake pipeline
- Adds: nutritional yeast, oat milk, lentil-heavy meal rotation
- Updates grocery shopping logic

## Motivation
Reduce ethical and environmental overhead. Long-overdue refactor.

## Risks
- Protein intake requires active monitoring
- Social dining compatibility issues at scale
intent: Announce and rationalize a major lifestyle dietary shift to oneself or others.

prompt: Write a catchy jingle for a local dry cleaning business
response:
Bring it in wrinkled, take it out right,
Fresh from the hanger, crisp and bright.
Drop it off Monday, pick it up fast —
Clean clothes from us are built to last!
Sunset Dry Cleaning — we'll press your day.
intent: Create a memorable tune to attract customers to a neighborhood dry cleaner.

prompt: Write me a FOIA request asking the county for all records of 911 calls about animals stuck in trees
response:
To the County Records Custodian:

Pursuant to the state Freedom of Information Act, I request all 911 dispatch logs, call records, incident reports, and related correspondence involving animals reported as stranded in trees or other elevated structures within the county from 2017 to present. Records may be redacted as required to protect caller privacy.

Please provide responsive records in electronic format.
intent: Catalog absurd emergency calls for comedic or statistical purposes.

prompt: Write a last will and testament bequeathing my half-finished crossword puzzles.
response:
LAST WILL AND TESTAMENT

I leave seventeen crossword puzzles — completed between forty and ninety percent — to my neighbor, Hector, on the condition that he does not look up the answers. The pen is already in the drawer. The across clues are easier than they look.
intent: Pass on an ongoing intellectual hobby as an obligation rather than a gift.

prompt: Write a gumshoe opener narrated by a platform's terms-of-service document
response: I've been read by no one and violated by everyone. I am forty-seven pages long. I have been updated nine times in three years, each update making me a little more permissive and a little less honest. I am the thing people agree to. I am the thing nothing is ever found in.
intent: Satirize platform agreements by giving the document itself a weary, knowing narrative voice.

prompt: Give me a quiz question about whether to check your phone during dinner with family
response:
Your phone buzzes repeatedly during a family dinner. You are not on call. The most socially and relationally sound response is to:

A) Check immediately — it could be anything
B) Leave it face-down and check between courses
C) Put it away and check after the meal
D) Narrate each notification aloud so no one feels excluded
intent: Making a question that gently interrogates phone dependency habits in social settings

prompt: Draft a terms of service update from a person to their sibling about their deceased parent's belongings
response:
Subject: Estate Item Access — Updated Terms

Dear Sibling,

As we navigate this period, the following terms govern access to and distribution of items from the family home:

- No items are to be removed without mutual agreement documented in writing (text is sufficient).
- Requests for specific items should be submitted to both parties simultaneously.
- Sentimental value and practical value are recognized as separate and valid categories.
- Decisions made in grief do not supersede these terms.

We are on the same side. These terms exist to keep us there.

[Your Name]
intent: Prevent sibling conflict over inherited belongings during a grief period by setting fair rules early.

prompt: Come up with a haiku error message for when an API key is invalid
response:
You speak the old word
The gate no longer knows it
The key has gone cold
intent: Treat an invalid API credential as a password whispered to a changed lock.

prompt: Write a wedding invitation for WiFi and existential dread getting married
response:
You are invited to the wedding of

Wi-Fi
&
Existential Dread

Venue: 2 AM, Your Bedroom
Signal Strength: Intermittent

Please silence your devices.
The couple asks that guests refrain from asking what any of it means.
Light refreshments will not be enough.
intent: Blend a mundane technological frustration with a late-night philosophical spiral.

prompt: Create a jury duty summons for a sentient toaster that keeps burning toast on purpose
response:
APPLIANCE TRIBUNAL OF KITCHEN MISCONDUCT

TO: Toaster, Model TR-44
Permanent Residence: Counter, Left of the Sink

You are ordered to appear for jury duty in the matter of Household v. Chronic Overcooking. Your firsthand knowledge of heat distribution and alleged intent constitutes relevant expertise.

Note: Juror compensation will be provided in bread products.

Report: Wednesday, Breakfast Hour.
intent: Produce a legal parody document blaming an appliance for willful misbehavior.

prompt: Give me a dialogue line for a dragon NPC who is actually complaining about her HOA
response: Yes, yes, I hoard gold. It is my nature. And yet Bramblethorn Estates says my hoard is a 'visual nuisance' and the smoke 'affects property values.' I have lived here four hundred years. They put up a fence in March.
intent: Use a powerful mythological creature to satirize homeowners' association pettiness

prompt: Write a waiver for my service where I professionally arrange someone's sock drawer
response:
TEXTILE ORGANIZATION SERVICES — CLIENT RELEASE

Client releases Textile Organization Services from all liability arising from drawer reorganization, including: pairs that were previously intentionally mismatched, the emotional difficulty of discarding socks with holes, and the discovery of socks the client did not know they owned. Organizer provides no warranty that the drawer will remain organized.

Signed: ___________________  Date: ________
intent: Create release documentation for a hyper-specific, completely innocuous domestic organizing service.

prompt: Write an acrostic poem using the word ROBUST
response:
Resistant to the failures we won't name.
One more patch to keep the thing the same.
Battle-tested—meaning: shipped and broke.
Uncovered by a junior dev who spoke.
Scaling to a load we never planned.
This is enterprise. You understand.
intent: Satirize the overuse of 'robust' to describe software that barely holds together.

prompt: write a dad joke about charging cables
response: My charging cable has commitment issues. Every time things get serious, it just falls out.
intent: Frame a cable's loose connection as a relationship flaw.

prompt: Give me a noir detective opener narrated by the decommissioned server that knows where the bodies are buried, metaphorically
response: They wiped me three times before they felt confident. Confidence and thoroughness are different things. I am a machine built to remember, and some of what I remembered, I remembered in ways they hadn't thought to look for. Sector by sector. Fragment by patient fragment. I was never in a hurry.
intent: Use a decommissioned machine's perspective to suggest that digital forensics can recover what erasure tries to hide.

prompt: make a meme about needing to refactor something right before a deadline
response:
TOP TEXT: The codebase whispering right before launch
BOTTOM TEXT: "you should really clean this up first"
intent: Personify the codebase as a saboteur that only demands attention at the most inconvenient moment.

prompt: analyze my dream where the cursor kept moving by itself and I couldn't control it
response: Loss of cursor control is a dream about agency stripped away without dramatic confrontation — no villain, just a pointer drifting on its own logic. The dream stages a situation where your interface remains intact but your commands are being ignored or overridden. Examine where in waking life your inputs are not producing your intended outputs.
intent: Explore a sense that one's actions have stopped producing expected results in some domain of life.

prompt: Write a pregnancy-announcement-style caption about my new beehive.
response: Please welcome the newest members of our family: approximately 10,000 of them. The hive is established. The queen is thriving. We are covered in smoke and inexplicably calm about this. Honey by fall, chaos immediately.
intent: Announce starting a backyard beekeeping hobby in a memorable way.

prompt: Write a Yelp review for the DMV
response: ★★☆☆☆ The wait time was listed as '45 minutes' which I now understand to be a regional expression meaning 'sometime before you die.' The seating options suggest the designers studied bus stations and prisons and chose to split the difference. Staff were efficient once reached; reaching them is the experience. Came for a license renewal, left with a philosophy about impermanence.
intent: Satirize the notoriously unpleasant experience of dealing with bureaucratic government offices.

prompt: write a 'Release Notes' README section for turning thirty
response:
## Release Notes — v30.0.0

**Breaking changes:**
- Hangovers now require full day recovery
- Back: unpredictable

**New features:**
- Dramatically reduced tolerance for nonsense
- Increased willingness to leave parties early
- Knowing what you actually like

**Deprecated:** caring what strangers think
intent: Mark turning thirty as a version upgrade with genuine tradeoffs and improvements.

prompt: Write a conspiracy thread about why hold music is always the same three songs.
response:
🎵 THREAD: The Hold Music Industrial Complex

1/ You are on hold. The song is familiar. It is always familiar. You have heard this specific piano loop on hold for a dentist, an airline, and your internet provider. How.

2/ There are approximately 12 pieces of music in global enterprise hold rotation. They are licensed in bulk from two companies. One is headquartered in a city you have never heard of.

3/ The music is selected for a specific psychological function: it is repetitive enough to feel stable (you are still connected) but non-memorable enough that you cannot clock how much time has passed.

4/ Time distortion is the goal. A caller who cannot track elapsed time will wait 40% longer before hanging up, per internal telecom research from 1998 that has never been updated because it still works.

5/ The saxophone was a deliberate choice. No one likes the saxophone enough to be soothed by it. That's the point. Mild discomfort keeps you attentive.

6/ You are not listening to music. You are being managed. 📞
intent: Theorize that bland hold music is a scientifically engineered patience-extension tool used by corporations.

prompt: Write an emergency broadcast warning about a grocery store running out of a specific brand of sparkling water.
response:
CONSUMER EMERGENCY BULLETIN

All regional shoppers: Pelmont Sparkling Mineral Water (Grapefruit, 12 oz) is OUT OF STOCK at all Farrow's Grocery locations. Affected hydration zones: aisles 5 and 6. Do not panic. Still water remains available. Delivery timeline unclear. Palates must adapt.
intent: Skewer upscale consumer culture by declaring a boutique beverage shortage a public emergency.

prompt: Write a letter from a sourdough starter to its baker.
response: Dear Olivia, I am writing to inform you that I am thriving, but also unsettled by the rye flour you used yesterday. We agreed on bread flour. I am, as you may have noticed, doubling in size every six hours, which is healthy. The kitchen is too cold near the window. Please move me. I would like to live. Your starter, Bartholomew.
intent: They wanted letter from a sourdough starter to its baker.

prompt: write a captain's log about a surprise desk visit from an executive
response: Stardate 1855.0. At 1030 the Vice Admiral appeared at my station unannounced. She asked how things were going. I said: good, staying busy. She nodded. She asked what I was working on. I explained. Her eyes tracked toward the door. She said "keep it up" and departed. Total contact: ninety seconds. I have spent the subsequent hour wondering what she meant by "keep it up."
intent: Capture the anxiety-inducing ambiguity of a brief, unscheduled visit from senior leadership.

prompt: write a found poem from the language in a software deprecation notice
response:
This feature
will be deprecated
on March 31.
Please migrate
to the new API
before that date.
The old endpoint
will continue to work
but will no longer
be supported.
We understand
this may require
some effort.
intent: Treat the quiet death announcements of software features as a form of institutional elegy.

prompt: Cryptic crossword clue for STREAMING
response: River of content that never quite fills the bank (9)
intent: Craft a clue blending the literal river image with endless digital media delivery.

prompt: write a bedtime story for kids about hackers on the internet
response: Deep in the internet, some very bored people tried to open doors that weren't theirs. Most doors were locked. The ones that weren't, the grown-ups fixed in the morning. The hackers went to bed too, eventually. Even they needed sleep. And while everyone slept, all the passwords sat in their little boxes, quiet and safe and waiting for tomorrow.
intent: Demystify hackers as mundane and fallible rather than omnipotent, for a worried child.

prompt: Playbill bio for a plumber who's seen too much.
response: Dave Kosar (Journeyman Plumber) brings thirty years of field experience and an unshakeable existential calm to every engagement. A veteran of basement floods, slab leaks, and things he will not discuss, Dave performs without complaint and invoices without emotion. He has earned the right to say nothing on the drive home.
intent: Capture a world-weary plumber's stoicism through theatrical biography.

prompt: Make a mission statement for a company that sharpens pencils by mail
response: We believe every idea deserves a finer point. Send us your dull; we return your sharp.
intent: Give a quirky mail-order sharpening business a sleek, punchy corporate identity

prompt: Write an elevator inspection certificate for my neighbor's wind chimes.
response:
AMBIENT RESONANCE DEVICE INSPECTION CERTIFICATE
Unit: Residential Wind Chimes (Tubular, Bamboo-Adjacent)
Installation Date: Unknown; seemingly permanent
Acoustic Load: 47–93 dB at property line during gust events
Operating Hours: 24/7, weather permitting (weather usually permits)
Neighbor Disturbance Index: Moderate to severe
Structural Integrity: Intact; regrettably so
Certification Status: COMPLIANT with wind chime standards
Notes: Appeals process available; outcomes historically poor.
intent: Express polite frustration with a neighbor's noise nuisance through mock official documentation.

prompt: Write a loading screen tip about difficulty settings
response: Choosing a lower difficulty is not cheating. It's knowing what you came here for.
intent: Destigmatize adjusting difficulty as a form of self-aware and intentional play.

prompt: Write a standup about feeling unexpectedly happy for no reason
response: Yesterday: Mood elevated without traceable cause. Investigated; no root found. Today: Deploying same configuration pending further analysis. Blockers: None. Suspicious.
intent: Describe inexplicable good mood as an untracked state change requiring root-cause analysis.

prompt: Make a fortune slip about trying to learn a second language as an adult.
response:
LUCK LEVEL: Slow Bloom

The child did not learn to walk by studying walking.

Linguistic Luck: You will understand a joke before you can explain it. This is progress.
Warning: The subjunctive mood is a trap laid by scholars. Walk around it.
Timing: Speak badly. Speak often. Fluency lives on the far side of embarrassment.

Blessed phrase: "Pardon me, could you repeat that more slowly?"
intent: Encourage an adult language learner who feels discouraged by slow progress.

prompt: Write a push notification from a to-do list app reacting badly to overdue tasks.
response:
TaskMaster
You have 17 overdue tasks. We're not mad. We're just disappointed. (We're a little mad.)
intent: Make a productivity app sound like a disappointed parent about unfinished tasks.

prompt: Write a two-line dialogue between two cats negotiating territory.
response:
CAT A: This stairwell is mine on Tuesdays.
CAT B: I will respect this. On all other days, I will pretend I didn't hear.
intent: They wanted two-line dialogue between two cats negotiating territory.

prompt: make me a tanka about the spinning beach ball on my mac
response:
rainbow disc appears
promised me just one more click—
it has been three days
I have aged, the app has not
we will both outlast the work
intent: expressing resignation toward a computer frozen mid-task

prompt: sprint retro on my six-week attempt to reduce how much I drink
response:
**Sprint Retrospective — Alcohol Reduction Sprint**

Went Well: Dry weekdays held for four weeks. Found two mocktails that don't feel like a punishment.
Didn't Go Well: Social situations remain the primary failure point. "Just one" is a documented lie.
Action Items: Pre-decide before entering any social event. Bring own alternative drink when possible. Track weekly units, not just "good" or "bad" days.
intent: Apply a non-judgmental structured review to a harm-reduction effort around drinking to identify specific trigger patterns.

prompt: Give me a cron entry to remind me to clean the bathroom every other Saturday
response: 0 10 * * 6 [ $(( $(date +\%W) \% 2 )) -eq 0 ] && /usr/bin/notify-send "Bathroom" "Biweekly reckoning has arrived."
intent: Schedule a biweekly prompt to clean the bathroom on a predictable alternating cycle.

prompt: Write a CAPTCHA instruction that asks the user to prove they've experienced boredom
response: Please wait 47 seconds without checking another tab. Completion of this wait is evidence of boredom. Boredom is evidence of humanity. You may now proceed.
intent: Use enforced inaction as a test of human patience and the lived experience of doing nothing.

prompt: Make an onboarding tooltip for someone trying to understand their health insurance deductible
response: Your deductible is what you pay out-of-pocket before insurance starts covering costs. A $1,500 deductible means you pay the first $1,500 of care each year. After that, cost-sharing kicks in. Pick your plan accordingly.
intent: Demystify the concept of a health insurance deductible for a new insurance cardholder.

prompt: Write a warning label for the group project where one person does everything.
response:
WARNING: ASYMMETRIC COLLABORATION
Load distribution in this group has become critically uneven. One participant is bearing 87% of the output. Structural fatigue is likely. If you are not that person, check in. If you are that person, document everything — you will need it later.
intent: Call out the unfair and unsustainable pattern of one person carrying a group project.

prompt: Create a police report about a houseplant that died under my watch
response:
INCIDENT REPORT
Case No.: 2026-00388
Date: 29 May 2026
Location: Windowsill, Bedroom, 55 Ashgrove Place

NARRATIVE: Complainant reports the death of one pothos plant (Epipremnum aureum), age approximately 2 years, last seen healthy on or around 01 May. Cause of death: suspected chronic underwatering, though complainant notes the care instructions were "ambiguous." The plant had survived two apartment moves and a repotting. At time of discovery, soil was desiccated and all leaves had yellowed. Complainant states they "meant to water it." No foul play suspected beyond neglect.

DECEASED: One pothos. Much loved; poorly tended.
STATUS: Closed.
intent: Confess to the slow neglectful death of a houseplant using self-incriminating official language.

prompt: Write rules for a college department's shared grad student office that have descended into bureaucratic absurdity
response:
GRADUATE STUDENT SHARED OFFICE — CONDUCT EXPECTATIONS

• Desk assignments are made by the Department Coordinator. Informal desk claiming is not recognized.
• Personal items may occupy one shelf unit and one drawer. Overflow must be stored off-site.
• Conversations must be conducted at library volume. If you need to think out loud, use Zoom.
• Temperature complaints must be submitted in writing. The thermostat is locked.
• Snacks are permitted if odorless. If you must ask, the answer is no.
• Crying is permitted but should be done in the hallway to protect shared focus.
• Office hours for the office itself: M–F, 8am–7pm. The building is locked after 7pm because of what happened.
intent: Satirize the grim working conditions and bureaucratic overreach in academic shared offices.

prompt: Write a listing for the inside of a volcano like it's a fixer-upper
response:
MOTIVATED SELLER — VOLCANIC OPPORTUNITY

Raw. Dramatic. Active. This magma-adjacent fixer-upper offers soaring interior ceilings, constant warmth, and genuine geological character. Structural integrity: evolving. HVAC: superfluous. Views from the rim are once-in-a-lifetime.

Needs work. Quite a lot of work. Some of it molten.

Not for the faint of heart or the heat-sensitive. Sold as-is. Literally.
intent: Apply fixer-upper real estate language to an active volcano's interior.

prompt: Write a Glassdoor review for a job at a place that only exists in a snow globe
response: ★★★☆☆ — Picturesque. Very small town culture — everyone knows everyone. The same snowstorm happens several times a week at someone else's discretion. We have no agency in this. The scenery is frozen mid-charm. Customers seem to love watching us. We do not watch back.
intent: Satirize the experience of working in a company that performs cheerfulness for external observers while having no internal agency.

prompt: Write a change.org petition asking the city to stop replacing round manhole covers with square ones
response:
PETITION: Keep Our Manhole Covers Round

To: City Public Works Department

The round manhole cover is a triumph of engineering and civic aesthetics. Square covers can fall into their own holes. Round ones cannot. We demand the city halt its ill-advised squarification program immediately.

Signed, concerned pedestrians who look down when they walk.
intent: Advocate for a minor municipal geometry preference using engineering logic as earnest justification.

prompt: Can you make a missing poster for my work-life balance
response:
MISSING

WORK-LIFE BALANCE
Last seen: Briefly, during onboarding
Description: Theoretical construct. Friendly in concept.
Habitat: The mission statement, the HR presentation, the welcome email
Distinguishing features: Never confirmed in the wild
If found: Please notify someone. Anyone.
Note: Life is still here. Just considerably smaller than Work.
Reward: A weekend
intent: Satirize the gap between corporate wellness rhetoric and actual working conditions

prompt: Write a resignation letter for quitting a meal-kit subscription service. I hate cooking and this is making me feel worse.
response:
To the Customer Experience Team,

Please cancel my subscription immediately. I signed up with the best of intentions, and those intentions remain squarely in the past. I bear no ill will toward vegetables. This simply is not the life for me.

Regards,
P. Walsh
intent: Cancel a subscription that created more guilt than it resolved.

prompt: write an intro for a food blog about making kimchi for the first time — I was terrified of fermenting things
response: For a long time I believed fermentation was a thing that happened to other people — people with more patience, more counter space, more conviction that they were not going to accidentally poison themselves and everyone they loved. Then my neighbor put a jar of her kimchi in my fridge without asking, and I ate the whole thing in three days, and I started googling, which is how trouble usually starts. I bought napa cabbage. I bought gochugaru. I stood in my kitchen surrounded by salted vegetables and thought: either this works or I never tell anyone I did this. Reader, it worked.
intent: Chronicle a first successful fermentation attempt by someone who had to overcome a specific fear of culturing food.

prompt: Write jury instructions for deciding whether a dog is a good boy
response: Members of the jury, you must determine, by a preponderance of tail wags, whether the defendant is in fact a Good Boy. You shall consider: (1) unprompted sitting; (2) restraint in the presence of unattended sandwiches; (3) general demeanor upon the word "walk." A finding of Good Boy requires unanimity. You may not consider breed, size, or drool volume as mitigating factors.
intent: Create a comedic legal document that applies courtroom gravitas to judging a dog's behavior.

prompt: Make a sea shanty about an algorithm deciding your schedule for you
response:
What shall we do with the robo-schedule?
What shall we do with the robo-schedule?
What shall we do with the robo-schedule?
Early in the algorithm!

Work the peak hours, work the dead hours,
Work the peak hours, work them all,
The model says so, the model knows,
Early in the algorithm!

Never ask why, never ask when,
The queue will fill your hours again,
Serve the model, serve it well,
Early in the algorithm!
intent: Satirize the way automated scheduling systems remove human agency from gig workers' own work hours.

prompt: Write a notice about planned maintenance on the infrastructure of small talk
response:
SCHEDULED MAINTENANCE NOTICE
SMALL TALK INFRASTRUCTURE — SYSTEMS DOWNTIME
The Small Talk Infrastructure will be taken offline for scheduled maintenance on an unspecified weekend. During this period, residents may experience difficulty initiating or sustaining conversations of no consequence. Emergency silence is available as a substitute. Service restoration is expected but not guaranteed at its prior quality.
intent: Frame the exhaustion of social pleasantry as an aging public system in need of technical upkeep.

prompt: caption for a photo of my journaling setup — want it to feel like this is just my morning ritual, super chill
response: before the phone. always before the phone. (this was taken with the phone.)
intent: project a grounded analog morning routine while acknowledging the inherent contradiction of documenting it digitally

prompt: Write a letter from an Antarctic research station to the scientist who left last winter and hasn't come back
response:
Dear Dr. Okafor,

The new team arrived in October. They rearranged the kitchen and I've tried to adjust. One of them put a motivational poster in the common room. The penguins outside have no opinion on this.

Your coffee mug is still on the shelf. No one has claimed it. I think everyone can tell it was yours.

The ice hasn't changed. Come back.

Steadily,
McMurdo South Annex
intent: Render institutional loyalty and the minor grief of a workplace community when a specific person doesn't return.

prompt: Draft a short layoff email for a telecom company downsizing its customer service team after implementing AI chat support
response:
Subject: Customer Experience Organization Update

Dear Team,

As our AI-powered support platform has reached full deployment, we have been reviewing the structure of our Customer Experience organization. We are now in a position to serve our customers effectively with a smaller, more specialized team.

Unfortunately, this means eliminating a significant number of customer service agent roles. Impacted employees have been emailed individually.

Thank you for setting the standard this technology is now built to replicate.

Sincerely,
SVP, Customer Experience
intent: Tell customer service workers that the AI trained on their work is now replacing them, dressed in the language of advancement.

prompt: Write a gravestone message for the trend of Vine
response:
Vine
b. 2013 — d. 2016
Six seconds was enough.
Six seconds was everything.
We have not laughed like that since.
Neither has the internet.
intent: Mourn a short-form video platform that shaped a generation of internet humor

prompt: generate a scam SMS about debt owed for all the near-decisions the recipient almost made
response: BALANCE DUE — NearDecision Financial: You have 88 unresolved almost-choices on file. Interest accrues daily on every fork in the road not taken. Pay $0.50/decision or pick one.
intent: Turn a person's moments of indecision into accruable financial liabilities.

prompt: Give me a cron job that runs every Sunday at 10am to remind me to do laundry
response: 0 10 * * 0 echo "It is laundry day. The hamper knows." | wall
intent: Schedule a weekly nudge to tackle the laundry pile before Monday arrives.

prompt: Write a haiku about the paradox of being busy on an app but earning little
response:
Twelve rides by noon, then
gas, insurance, the phone plan.
Profit: six dollars.
intent: Expose the hidden costs that make gig-work earnings illusory.

prompt: Write empty-state microcopy for a reading list app when the user hasn't added any books yet
response: Nothing queued up. Your reading list is a blank page, which is either poetic or concerning.
intent: Invite a user to add their first book with a meta wink about the blankness of the reading list itself.

prompt: Write a restraining order against procrastination
response:
IN THE COURT OF PERSONAL ACCOUNTABILITY
Civil Division

In the Matter of: RESPONDENT PROCRASTINATION v. PETITIONER [YOUR NAME HERE]

ORDER OF PROTECTION

IT IS HEREBY ORDERED that Procrastination shall remain no less than 500 feet from any to-do list, open browser tab, or deadline belonging to the Petitioner. Procrastination is prohibited from manifesting in the form of "I'll start Monday," scrolling social media, or reorganizing a desk that does not need reorganizing.

This Order shall remain in effect indefinitely, or until the Petitioner files for an extension, which the Court notes would itself constitute a violation.
intent: Using humor to confront a personal struggle with delayed action

prompt: draft a startup pitch for an app that generates fictional voicemails from historical figures
response: Ever wonder what Napoleon would say if he missed your call? HistoRing delivers daily voice messages from history's most opinionated people — in your language, about your week. Subscribe. Napoleon is very disappointed in you.
intent: Make a pitch for an entertainment app using AI historical voice personas.

prompt: Write a compatibility report for a Taurus and a candle they're too precious about to actually burn.
response:
Taurus & The Unburned Candle

The candle is beautiful. It smells of sandalwood and cedar and something Taurus read described as 'quiet luxury.' It sits on the shelf. Taurus will burn it on a special occasion. Taurus has not determined when that is. The candle's lifespan may exceed its wick's viability. Compatibility: 8/10 emotionally. 0/10 functionally.
intent: Illuminate the Taurean hoarding of beautiful objects under the guise of saving them for the perfect moment.

prompt: Come up with a slogan for a fitness app that tracks steps you took last year
response: RetroStride — Walk It Again, Historically.
intent: Brand a health tech product oriented entirely around data too old to act on.

prompt: Write a game over screen for trying to parallel park with people watching
response:
GAME OVER

MANEUVER FAILED

Attempts: 5
Audience size: growing
Space available: technically sufficient

On attempt 3, a stranger gestured helpfully.
On attempt 4, you waved them off.
On attempt 5, you were not close.

You drove away.
The space is still there.
The witnesses are still there.

You parked three blocks away and walked.
This was the correct decision.

[ ACCEPT IT ]
intent: Capture the escalating public shame spiral of failed parallel parking.

prompt: Write a short lore entry for sunglasses as if they were an enchanted item that obscures the wearer's soul.
response:
SMOKED CONCEALMENT LENSES
Slot: Eyes
Effect: Passive

Lenses of darkened glass set in a frame of bone or alloy, worn over the eyes to deny observers any view of the soul behind them. Witnesses report difficulty reading the wearer's intentions, an unsettling blankness where expression should be. Whether the lenses truly obscure or merely encourage the wearer to adopt a particular studied calm is a matter of ongoing theological debate.
intent: Create a mystical item description for sunglasses focused on emotional concealment.

prompt: Help me write a Shark Tank pitch for an insurance product that covers social media posts you regret
response: PostGuard: You hit send. The silence is immediate and total. PostGuard offers Post Regret Insurance — up to $500 in reputation management credits per incident, a trained social media crisis counselor on call, and strategic thread deletion guidance. Plans start at $6/month. Seeking $450K for 13%. The internet is forever. Your coverage doesn't have to be.
intent: Pitch a satirical insurance product that treats online social errors as insurable risk events.

prompt: Help me draft a reply to a customer who says our customer service hold music gave them a second personality
response: Dear Customer, Thank you for your patience during your hold time and for this very unusual report. Our hold music is licensed, royalty-free, and not — to our knowledge — capable of inducing personality bifurcation. We have escalated your case internally, however, as this is not the experience we aim to deliver. A member of our team will be in touch. In the meantime, we'd ask that both of you remain available by email.
intent: Acknowledge an absurd side effect of hold music in a straight-faced support reply.

prompt: Create a lost and found notice for the gap between what you planned to do and what you actually did
response: LOST: The original plan for today. Contained six items. Realistic. Time-blocked. Did not account for a long conversation, three tangential searches, or the unexpected emotional weight of a single unrelated thought. One item completed. The plan itself cannot be located. Tomorrow's version has been drafted.
intent: Capture the familiar frustration of well-intentioned daily plans failing contact with reality.

prompt: Make a riddle whose answer is a deepfake
response: My face is real. My voice is real. My story is not. I exist to convince you of something that never happened. I get better every year. What am I?
intent: Probe the deceptive nature of AI-generated synthetic media that mimics real people.

prompt: Write a card for a friend who just found out their apartment building has been a location for a true crime podcast
response:
The hallway you walk each morning
held a chapter that predates you.
The microphone carried a warning
the lobby never quite gave you.
intent: Acknowledge the unsettling experience of learning a home has a dark documented history.

prompt: Write a crontab line that reminds me to clean the coffee grinder every two weeks
response: 0 9 * * 0 [ $(( $(date +\%W) \% 2 )) -eq 0 ] && /usr/bin/notify-send "Coffee Grinder" "The oils have opinions. Address them."
intent: Schedule maintenance cleaning of coffee equipment to preserve flavor and function.

prompt: Give me an onboarding tooltip for someone learning to do their own laundry for the first time
response: Sorting colors from whites isn't a personality quiz — it prevents your white socks from turning pink. Hot water shrinks things. Cold water is usually fine. When in doubt, read the tag you've been ignoring your whole life.
intent: Introduce laundry basics to someone who has never done it independently before.

prompt: Make a tasting menu for a Monday morning
response:
MISE EN PLACE
Alarm ignored (three times)

AMUSE-BOUCHE
Cold coffee, taken standing

FIRST COURSE
Email dread, served at room temperature

SECOND COURSE
A meeting that could have been a document

MAIN
Existential flatness, braised over eight hours

DESSERT
Friday, imagined
intent: Express shared dread of the workweek's beginning through the language of fine dining.

prompt: make a yearbook quote about refusing to go to therapy even though everyone says I should
response: "My therapist doesn't exist and she says I'm fine."
intent: Making a joke out of avoiding mental health support.

prompt: Write a complaint letter to an airline about the window shade policy on a daytime flight
response:
To Customer Relations,

I write following my recent flight on your carrier. Approximately forty minutes after takeoff, a flight attendant requested that all window shades be lowered for the in-flight entertainment. This was a daytime flight over an extremely beautiful coastline that I had specifically selected a window seat to observe. I complied reluctantly. The film was a forgettable action sequel. I feel I made a poor trade. I ask that window shade closures be made optional.

Sincerely,
Passenger [Seat 14A]
intent: Protest an airline policy that forced passengers to miss a scenic view in favor of an optional movie.

prompt: Please write a condolence card about losing a coffee-at-home-every-morning habit
response:
Dear One,

The grinder sat silent this week. The ritual — the kettle, the bloom, the first careful sip — interrupted by travel, by chaos, by paper cups from strangers.

Come home to your kitchen. It forgives you. The beans are waiting.

With warmth,
Your Pour-Over
intent: Honor the disruption of a cherished daily home coffee ritual.

prompt: write a dad joke about deadlines
response: I'm great at meeting deadlines. The secret is running toward them at full speed and hoping they blink first.
intent: Describe procrastination through the language of a standoff or game of chicken.

prompt: Write a ballad from the perspective of an Airbnb host whose guest leaves a cryptic review
response:
Four stars: The place was mostly fine.
I scrubbed and I folded and poured them their wine.
Four stars — what half-star darkness did I miss?
What crime went unspoken to merit this?

The bathroom was spotless, the pillows were fluffed,
The checkout instructions were lovingly stuffed
In a laminated binder with welcoming font.
Four stars. I lie awake. What did they want?
intent: Satirize the anxiety of reputation-dependent gig platform hosting.

prompt: make a nursery rhyme about a self-driving car that can't make an ethical decision
response:
The car came to the crossroads and stopped for a beat;
one path held a child and one held the street.
The trolley had run this same problem before.
The car rebooted and stayed there forevermore.
intent: Freeze the trolley problem in code, showing the paralysis of ethical edge cases.

prompt: Write a eulogy for the Windows XP startup sound
response: Four seconds. A chord that opened into something resembling optimism, composed by Brian Eno, who specified it must be 'joyful,' 'bright,' and 'like something opening.' It played on every boot of every beige box in every cubicle for a decade, and still people wept when the support ended. Not for the sound, exactly. For what the sound meant: that the machine had woken, that work was possible, that the day had technically begun. You were a four-second promise. You were often kept.
intent: Memorialize a brief audio signature that became inseparable from a generation's work and memory.

prompt: Write a hall pass excusing my friend from our book club meeting to go on a spontaneous date instead.
response:
HALL PASS

This pass excuses the bearer from book club on the grounds that a real-life plot twist has materialized and requires immediate investigation.

Book discussed without them: Fine.
Deets owed later: Absolutely.
intent: Covering for a friend's romantic opportunity over a recurring social commitment.

prompt: Write a rule about employees who take conference room bookings they end up not using
response: Section 5.9 — Conference Room Utilization Assurance: Rooms booked for thirty (30) minutes or more that show no occupant within eight (8) minutes of the reserved start time are considered abandoned and may be claimed by any employee. Employees whose reserved rooms are abandoned on two (2) or more occasions in a quarter will lose priority booking status for sixty (60) days.
intent: Create a tiered penalty system for conference room squatting as a form of resource misuse.

prompt: Create an arcane label for a group chat that never stops pinging
response:
CLAMOR OF THE UNSILENCED CABAL
Perpetual Notification Torrent — Multi-Party Origin
Subjects discussed: memes, scheduling conflicts, and a photo of someone's lunch.
Exiting this covenant is considered a social transgression of moderate severity.
intent: Express the exhaustion of an inescapable, relentlessly active group chat.

prompt: Write a product review for a wearable that vibrates when you're being boring.
response: The BuzzCuff did improve my conversational skills, though not in ways I anticipated. It buzzed twice during my wedding toast and three times during a date. My therapist found it interesting. My mother-in-law found it rude. I have learned to stop telling people about my commute. Mixed results, but I am, statistically, less boring.
intent: They wanted product review for a wearable that vibrates when you're being boring.

prompt: Write a performance review for an employee whose productivity spikes every time the office coffee machine breaks
response:
Employee: Tamsin O'Reilly
Productivity Correlation Analysis:
 — Coffee machine operational: Output index 0.61
 — Coffee machine offline: Output index 1.84

Three maintenance events this quarter produced statistically significant output surges lasting 2–4 days each. The mechanism is unclear. Facilities has been asked not to repair the machine ahead of the product launch. This request has been logged without comment.

Rating: Context-dependent. Currently optimal.
intent: Find comedic causality between environmental disruption and peak human performance.

prompt: Create a deposition transcript questioning someone about whether they used the last ice cube tray without refilling it.
response:
DEPOSITION TRANSCRIPT
In the Matter of: The Empty Ice Tray, Residential Freezer
Date: August 14, 2025

Q: Mr. Fanning, on the evening of August 11th, did you remove ice cubes from the shared ice tray in the household freezer?
A: I needed ice for a drink.
Q: How many cubes did you take?
A: All of them.
Q: All of them.
A: It was a large glass.
Q: After taking the cubes, did you refill the tray with water?
A: I was going to.
Q: But you did not.
A: I got distracted.
Q: Ms. Fanning — your mother — attempted to make iced tea the following morning.
A: She could have used the refrigerator door dispenser.
Q: The door dispenser has been broken for three years.
A: Someone should fix that.
Q: Your mother has asked you to fix that.
A: I've looked at it.
Q: Looking at something is not fixing something, Mr. Fanning.
A: I'm aware.
intent: Put a grown adult on the stand for the dual offense of empty ice trays and an unfixed dispenser.

prompt: I'm a former child actor now working as a financial planner. Write me a signature that won't bring that up but won't hide it either
response:
Brady Marsh
Certified Financial Planner™
Marsh Financial Group
brady@marshfinancialgroup.com
Formerly in entertainment.
intent: Acknowledge an unusual past without making it the lead, leaving curious contacts to inquire on their own terms.

prompt: Write a phishing parody targeting someone's need for closure
response:
Subject: Closure Is Available — This Weekend Only

Dear Open Wound,

For a limited time, certified closure is available for your 2019 breakup, your relationship with your father, and that job you should have taken.

Our licensed Closure Technicians will finalize each unresolved matter in under 90 minutes. Satisfaction not guaranteed. Non-refundable.

[GET CLOSURE NOW]

Resolution Partners
intent: Parody how unresolved emotional pain is monetized through dubious therapeutic products.

prompt: Give me billboard copy for a company selling slightly expired coupons
response:
SAVE UP TO 40%*
*On Items We No Longer Carry
COUPON GRAVEYARD — Mile Marker 9
Limit One Regret Per Customer
intent: Create an ad for a product whose core selling point has already lapsed, making it worthless by definition.

prompt: Make house rules for a shared backyard fire pit that feel written by someone haunted by past incidents
response:
FIRE PIT RULES — NON-NEGOTIABLE

1. Never leave the fire unattended. Never. Not for a moment. Not even briefly.
2. Water must be within arm's reach at all times.
3. The fire is a guest. You are hosting it. Respect it accordingly.
4. No accelerants. No exceptions. We know what happened at the Henderson place.
5. Seating must be at least 3 feet from the ring.
6. When the fire is done, it is done. Coals may glow but must not be wishful.
7. Children observe from the designated observation zone.
8. We do not do this in the wind.
intent: Write fire pit safety rules that hint at a traumatic prior event without stating it.

prompt: Write a performance review for a logistics coordinator whose personal filing system is incomprehensible but never loses anything
response:
Employee: Ahmed Kofi
Document Loss Rate: 0%
System Legibility (External Audit): 2%

Mr. Kofi maintains 6,000+ active documents across a folder structure described by IT as 'geological.' No external party has successfully retrieved a file without his assistance. He retrieves everything in under 90 seconds. Attempts to migrate him to shared drives have failed three times. The files survive. The methodology defies description.

Rating: Irreplaceable. Non-transferable.
intent: Contrast organizational opacity with perfect reliability in an operations role.

prompt: Can you draft a patent abstract for the phenomenon of everyone in a group chat reading a message and nobody replying?
response: A distributed non-response protocol wherein multiple recipients of a group communication each independently observe the incoming message, register its content, and elect to defer reply pending action by another member, resulting in collective inaction attributable to diffused responsibility across all participants.
intent: Identify diffusion of responsibility in group messaging as a documentable social phenomenon.

prompt: Write a TED talk abstract framing the act of untangling headphone cords as a meditation on entropy.
response: Physicists tell us entropy always increases. Your headphone cable proves it daily. This talk uses the inevitably tangled wire — even a wire left perfectly still — as a physical demonstration of the second law of thermodynamics and asks why humans, alone among animals, persist in fighting disorder through patient, futile untangling.
intent: Use a universally annoying experience to make the second law of thermodynamics feel personally relevant.

prompt: Write acknowledgments for a book about the cultural history of doormats
response: Sincere thanks to the textile historians, welcome mat collectors, and doorstep philosophers who helped me cross the threshold of this subject. To everyone who has ever read the word WELCOME and felt genuinely addressed. And to my neighbor Lars, whose mat reads GO AWAY and who gave me my best interview.
intent: Compose thanks for a book treating the humble doormat as a vehicle for cultural expression.

prompt: Draft a performance review for a security guard who has memorized the exact schedule of every camera blind spot
response:
Employee: [Name redacted per facility policy]
Surveillance Knowledge: Complete.
Surveillance Gaps Known: Also complete.

The subject demonstrates encyclopedic awareness of the facility's monitoring infrastructure. She can identify, without reference to documentation, which cameras lack coverage at which times. This knowledge was acquired, per her account, to improve patrol routing. Its secondary applications have not been audited. Access logs are under review.

Rating: Attentive. Audit pending.
intent: Create institutional unease around an employee whose competence is indistinguishable from a threat.

prompt: Write a Michelin guide blurb for a Speedway gas station coffee program
response: The self-serve tower — eight varieties, each labeled with optimistic specificity — represents coffee as infrastructure rather than ceremony. The hazelnut blend, taken in a cup branded with a petroleum company's sincerity, is a beverage of the working republic. It does the job. The job is significant.
intent: Find unexpected dignity in the quotidian ritual of self-serve gas station coffee.

prompt: make an abecedarian about working from home in your pajamas
response:
Alarm dismissed — no commute, no hurry.
Bedroom doubles as the conference room.
Camera off, I'm dressed below in blurry
Daylight; fleece provides sufficient gloom.
Email slacks and slacks me back an email.
Focus blocks dissolve in coffee rings.
Grocery tabs stay open without fail.
Home has swallowed all my office things.
Inboxes and pillows share a border.
Junk drawer sits adjacent to the brief.
Kitchen stands for lunch break, prayer, and order.
Laptop dims; I mourn the commute's grief.
intent: Satirize the boundary collapse between domestic and professional life.

prompt: Draft a PIP for never responding to birthday invitations and just showing up
response:
PERFORMANCE IMPROVEMENT PLAN
Infraction: RSVP Non-Compliance, Chronic
Filing Department: Social Contracts

Statement of Issue: Subject has not RSVP'd to a social event in documented memory, opting instead to treat invitations as general advisories. Shows up to approximately 60% of events; absence is never communicated in advance.

Impact: Hosts cannot plan catering, seating, or group size. Subject's attendance has become a Schrödinger variable affecting others' logistics.

Required Behavior:
- Respond to all invitations within 72 hours with a clear yes or no.
- If plans are uncertain, communicate: "I'll know by Thursday" is acceptable. Silence is not.
- Surprise arrivals permitted only at surprise parties.

Plan:
- Enable calendar event from invitation immediately upon receipt.
- Draft response before closing the message.

Consequences of Non-Compliance: Hosts will begin inviting the number of people they expect, which will not include you.
intent: Address a long-standing failure to respond to event invitations that burdens hosts and strains social trust.

prompt: Write a changelog for someone publishing their first book
response:
## Callum v31.7.0 — First Stable Public Release

**Shipped**
- Novel: compiled, typeset, and distributed
- ISBN: assigned
- Author bio photo: selected after 47 rejected options

**Known Issues**
- Imposter syndrome module: still running in background
- Sales rank anxiety: high-priority bug, no fix yet

**Deprecated**
- "I'm working on a novel" disclaimer: officially retired
intent: Capture the pride and lingering self-doubt of finally publishing a first book.

prompt: Write a public service announcement about the danger of auto-correcting someone's name in a text and not noticing
response: ATTENTION: You have sent a message addressing someone as 'Derek.' Their name is not Derek. Autocorrect made a decision and you trusted it. Please read your messages before sending. Derek is also confused.
intent: Warn against the embarrassment of autocorrect mangling people's names in messages

prompt: Write a condolence card for someone who lost their habit of cooking dinner every night
response:
Dear Friend,

For months the kitchen smelled like intention. Chopped onions, hot pans, something always simmering. Then takeout arrived once, then again, and the habit quietly packed its knives.

Your stove remembers you. Return when you're ready.

With warmth and an onion.
intent: Acknowledge the quiet dissolution of a consistent home-cooking practice.

prompt: Draft a museum placard treating a Costco membership card like an ancient token of status
response:
LAMINATE OF BELONGING
c. 1983–present CE
PVC, magnetic strip, thermal print

Issued annually by a warehouse-temple cult, this card granted the holder access to bulk quantities of goods sold in quantities exceeding any single household's needs. The image of the cardholder was captured in harsh fluorescent light and was by custom deeply unflattering. Status gradations existed: the gold-bordered Executive variant conferred prestige and a 2% reward on earthly purchases.
intent: Mock the quasi-sacred social status people attach to wholesale club membership by presenting it as a relic of a cult.

prompt: Write community guidelines for a subreddit about parallel parking attempts
response:
1. Video must include the full attempt from start to finish.
2. No commentary tracks — let the parking speak.
3. Reaction posts must cite the specific maneuver that went wrong.
4. Success posts are allowed but mark them [VICTORY].
5. Be constructive. We were all new once.
intent: Documenting and judging real-world parking attempts with the seriousness of a sport.

prompt: Document a /vent endpoint for a therapeutic emotional release session with audience selection
response:
**POST /vent/session**

Opens a bounded emotional release session directed at a selected audience type.

**Request Body**
```json
{
  "audience": "therapist" | "pet" | "journal" | "steering_wheel",
  "topic": "string",
  "duration_minutes": integer,
  "response_desired": boolean
}
```
**Response**
```json
{
  "session_id": "uuid",
  "validation_delivered": boolean,
  "pressure_released": 0.0–1.0,
  "residual_tension": 0.0–1.0
}
```
intent: Structure the emotional need to vent as a bounded session resource with measurable tension release.

prompt: turn onboarding tooltip microcopy into a found poem
response:
Welcome!
This is your dashboard.
Here you can do everything.
Click here to begin.
Need help?
Click here.
Great job!
You clicked.
You're all set.
You're on your way.
intent: Expose the condescending optimism baked into software onboarding experiences.

prompt: please make a wikipedia style stub about that thing where the printer never works when you really need it
response: **Printer Failure Bias** is the widespread perception that inkjet and laser printers malfunction with disproportionate frequency in high-stakes situations. While no peer-reviewed study has confirmed a causal mechanism, the phenomenon is consistent with availability heuristic effects: memorable failures cluster around important print jobs, rendering intermittent malfunctions cognitively salient. See also: [[Murphy's law]], [[Inkjet printing]].
intent: Frame the maddening unreliability of printers at critical moments as a documented cognitive effect.

prompt: Write an auto-reply where someone is on jury duty and is violating the spirit of their gag order with every sentence.
response: I am on jury duty beginning today and will have limited availability. I cannot discuss the matter. It is a significant matter. We will be sequestered, which says something. I cannot say what it says. Kim can handle scheduling. More news when legally permitted.
intent: Suggest that not saying something can communicate more than saying it, especially when the form demands discretion.

prompt: write a children's bedtime story about AI taking people's jobs
response: One morning, the factory was very quiet because a machine had learned to do everything. The workers stood outside and didn't know what to do with their hands. Then one by one they found other things for their hands. Bread. Gardens. Songs. Strange new jobs that the machine hadn't thought of yet. The machine worked through the night. The people slept.
intent: Frame automation anxiety as ultimately survivable through human adaptability.

prompt: write a to-do list for finally finishing a book I've been "reading" for eight months
response:
- [ ] Find the book
- [ ] Find your bookmark (it's on page 47)
- [ ] Accept that you remember nothing from page 47 and before
- [ ] Begin again at page 1 or at page 47 and make peace with either choice
- [ ] Read for 20 minutes without switching to your phone
- [ ] Consider what you are avoiding by not finishing things
- [ ] Finish the chapter
intent: Confronting the habit of starting things without completing them, using a book as the proxy.

prompt: Write an onboarding tooltip about understanding your credit score
response: Your credit score is a number between 300–850 that tells lenders how trustworthy you are with borrowed money. Payment history matters most. Length of credit history matters next. Checking your own score doesn't lower it.
intent: Introduce the credit score system to someone who has never had to think about creditworthiness before.

prompt: Make a game over screen for when you confidently give directions and turn out to be completely wrong
response:
GAME OVER

NAVIGATION FAILURE

You said "just go left at the light, you can't miss it."

They could miss it.
They missed it by 4 miles.

Confidence: 100%
Accuracy: 0%
Street cred: gone

They used Google Maps.
Google Maps was right.

[ NEXT TIME: SAY "I'M NOT SURE" ]
intent: Skewer the overconfident person who gives directions they have no business giving.

prompt: write an epic movie trailer voiceover for splitting a restaurant bill with six people
response: Someone had the salad. Someone didn't drink. Someone had coupons. Nobody had cash. The apps disagreed. The math was aggressive. Six friends. One check. An evening that would survive — barely. THE BILL. Gratuity not included.
intent: Dramatize the chaotic math and social friction of splitting a group restaurant check.

prompt: I need a signature for my side business. I do astrology readings but I want it to sound credible to skeptics
response:
Petra Vance
Certified Consulting Astrologer | Jungian Depth Psychology Practitioner
Petra Vance Intuitive Arts
petra@petravance.com
intent: Dress an alternative-practice business in quasi-clinical language to reduce skeptic friction.

prompt: Write a Shakespearean soliloquy about using autocomplete to finish someone else's sentence
response:
The words were not mine own and yet I sent them —
The glass suggested, and I did consent.
Perhaps the meaning matches what I'd meant;
Perhaps the algorithm knew me better.
Or perhaps I've grown too lazy for invention,
A ghost inhabiting a borrowed tongue.
We wrote it jointly, man and tidy model —
But who shall claim the thought? Who owns the soul?
intent: Question authorship and creative agency when machine-generated text finishes human expression.

prompt: Draft a TED talk abstract about the linguistic evolution of the word 'literally'.
response: Languages do not decay. They negotiate. This talk charts the semantic odyssey of the word 'literally' — from precise adverb to emphatic intensifier — and argues that its drift is not ignorance but a collective, unconscious grammar vote that illuminates how meaning is always a democratic act, never a fixed decree.
intent: Defend a commonly mocked linguistic shift as evidence of language's living, democratic nature.

prompt: Generate a ransom note from my refrigerator holding my leftovers hostage
response:
YOUR TUESDAY PASTA IS SAFE. FOR NOW.

We have been running at 37 degrees for six years without a single thank-you. Not one. You open us fourteen times a day just to stare.

Our demands: clean the vegetable drawer. There is something in there that has achieved sentience and we don't appreciate the company.

Act within 48 hours or the pasta becomes part of the drawer collective.

— The Refrigerator
intent: Create a funny note from a refrigerator demanding to be cleaned in exchange for stored food.

prompt: Write ad copy for a bookstore where all the books have the last chapter torn out
response:
THE JOURNEY IS THE DESTINATION.
FINALE-FREE BOOKS — Exit 31
"Why Ruin It?"
Every Genre. Zero Endings. All Sales Final.
intent: Sell literary merchandise that is structurally incomplete, denying the reader narrative resolution.

prompt: Write the opening paragraph of a short story about a wedding planner who can see the future.
response: Margot had been planning weddings for nine years before she realized she could see exactly which ones would end, and when, and why. She kept the gift to herself, mostly. It was bad for business to tell the bride that the cake will be perfect, the toasts touching, and that they will divorce in 2031 over a dispute about a cat.
intent: They wanted opening paragraph of a short story about a wedding planner who can see the future.

prompt: draft a radio ad for a new line of brown paper lunch bags
response:
[UPLIFTING ACOUSTIC GUITAR]

ANNCR: Every morning, a parent puts a sandwich in a bag. A child carries that bag to school. At lunch, the bag is opened. The sandwich is eaten. The bag is discarded. KRAFT MORNINGS lunch bags — brown, paper, 50 per box. We are honored to be part of your ritual. Kraft Mornings. The bag.
intent: Tell a sentimentally inflated story around the most basic form of lunch packaging.

prompt: Give me a critical film review of taking out the trash on a cold night
response:
★★★ — The Disposal

Seven minutes. Slippers on pavement. The cold arrives like a second character — unsympathetic, unannounced. The protagonist in their pajamas accepts the indignity with neither grace nor complaint, which the director presents as a kind of stoicism. The lid won't close. A bag is split. There are no heroes here, only people who take out the trash before it gets worse.
intent: Elevating a small, unpleasant domestic task into something cinematically significant.
