Your sandwich: reclassified as "theirs"

You were warned about the birds.
You were warned.

The bird did not eat all of it.
The bird left the crust.
The crust is an insult.

Beach enjoyment remaining: diminished
Respect for gulls: ironically increased

[ RETREAT INLAND ]
intent: Frame losing food to a seagull or beach bird as a swift and humiliating tactical defeat.

prompt: Write a deposition transcript about someone being questioned over their habit of pressing a crosswalk button that was already lit.
response:
DEPOSITION TRANSCRIPT
In the Matter of: The Crosswalk, Junction of Maple and 4th
Date: October 9, 2025

Q: Ms. Thornton, on the morning of October 6th, did you approach the crosswalk at Maple and 4th?
A: I was crossing the street.
Q: Upon approaching the button panel, was the button already illuminated?
A: I'm not sure I checked.
Q: Witness Mr. Alvarez states the button was lit.
A: I press the button.
Q: Even when it's already pressed?
A: Pressing it again feels more certain.
Q: The system registers one press or many as equivalent.
A: I didn't know that.
Q: You've pressed that button every weekday for approximately three years.
A: I walk to work.
Q: Every time.
A: I can't be sure every time.
Q: Mr. Alvarez can.
A: He watches closely.
Q: He has a long commute. He watches the crosswalk.
A: That's his thing, then.
Q: Apparently.
intent: Subject the compulsive crosswalk-button re-presser to formal questioning about their ritual.

prompt: Draft a cookie consent banner about storing someone's attention
response: We use attention cookies to monitor where your eyes linger, what you almost clicked, and how long you stared blankly before scrolling. You can withdraw your attention at any time, though we may not notice. Accept | Decline | Drift Off
intent: Satirize surveillance capitalism by framing human attention as a trackable, storable resource.

prompt: Draft a rec letter for a high school student applying to college who is likeable but academically average
response:
To the Admissions Committee,

I have taught Brianna for two semesters and cannot overstate the warmth she brings to the classroom. She is universally liked by peers, supportive of group discussions, and unfailingly kind to everyone she encounters. Her academic performance places her solidly within the broad middle of her cohort, and her work reflects a steady and honest effort. Brianna will be a genuinely positive presence in any community she joins.

Her Teacher
intent: Write a college recommendation that leans on character because the academic record doesn't support more.

prompt: Write a FAQ entry about why my smart fridge keeps ordering things I didn't ask for
response:
**Why does my smart fridge keep ordering groceries I didn't request?**

Your refrigerator's AI anticipates needs based on consumption patterns, expiration scanning, and seasonal models. If it ordered oat milk and you're a dairy household, check the preference profile under Settings > Household Identity. You can disable autonomous purchasing entirely, though the fridge will passive-aggressively note every item you run out of.
intent: The person wants to explain or understand the frustrating autonomy of AI-enabled home appliances making purchases without consent.

prompt: Write a pen pal letter from a census form to a household that keeps leaving it blank
response:
Dear Residents of 114 Wren Street,

This is my third visit. I'm not sure what I've done to deserve this level of avoidance. I ask six questions. Six. I'm not asking for your diary.

I want to count you. That's it. You deserve to be counted. The roads near your neighborhood are built on data like mine. The schools. The hospitals.

I'm willing to try again. Just this once. Tell me how many of you there are.

Persistently,
American Community Survey Form B-7
intent: Make a civic appeal through bureaucratic personification, framing data collection as an act of recognition and care.

prompt: Write a formal eviction notice for negativity bias
response:
FORMAL NOTICE OF EVICTION

To: Negativity Bias
Long-term Tenant, cognitive architecture, upper floors

You have amplified every minor slight to structural scale while dismissing genuine good fortune as statistical noise. Your evolutionary lease has lapsed. Gratitude has submitted a competing application and scored higher on the habitability rubric. You have 30 days. Please do not booby-trap the exits.

— The Prefrontal Cortex
intent: Trying to retrain one's mind toward appreciation rather than dwelling on what goes wrong.

prompt: Write a performance bonus letter for an employee who used vacation days they had already accrued.
response:
Dear Xiomara,

Records confirm that during the week of May 19th, you utilized five (5) accrued vacation days for the purpose of not working.

You did not check email. You did not send "just one quick reply." You were, for a documented stretch, away.

This correct use of a benefit you had earned is celebrated with the Rest and Recovery Bonus: $50.

We were worried you'd never leave.

With relief,
Human Resources Wellbeing Department
intent: Satirize workplace cultures where taking earned vacation is treated as unusual or even daring.

prompt: write a sestina about the phenomenon of 'link rot' — old hyperlinks going dead
response:
I click the link and find the void instead:
404, the number for not here.
The article I wanted lived at that
address in 2011. The record
still exists in someone's footnote, still
looks authoritative in a citation

that no one checks. The citation
points toward the void instead
of evidence. It is still
numbered, still formatted, still here
in print — but the record
it references is gone. That

exact combination of letters: that
path was the citation's
full address. The record
lived there. Now instead
lives nothing. Not even here-
but-moved. Just gone. Still

the references proliferate. Still
cited, still quoted. That
there's nothing here
is not reflected in the citation.
The footnote points instead
to vacuum. The record

of the record is all that's record-
ed. The original is still
only as an absence instead
of a presence. That
is the shape of citation
in decay: the here-

not-here. I click here
twenty years on. The record
expected from the citation
lived somewhere. It's still
numbered. But that
path leads instead

to here: 404. Still, the citation
records the instead of that
which was here.
intent: Meditate on digital impermanence and the degradation of the web's institutional memory.

prompt: Write a game over screen for burning dinner right before guests arrive
response:
GAME OVER

CULINARY INCIDENT

Cook time: 45 min
Actual cook time applied: 90 min (unintentional)
Guests ETA: 8 minutes

The smoke alarm has opinions.
The pan has given up.
The pasta is a warning.

Options remaining:
  › Order pizza (admit defeat)
  › Serve it anyway (bold)
  › Claim it's "charred" (culinary)

[ CHOOSE YOUR ENDING ]
intent: Frame the disaster of a burnt dinner right before hosting as a culinary game-over moment.

prompt: Can you do a game over screen for when you step on a Lego in the dark
response:
GAME OVER

ENVIRONMENTAL HAZARD DETECTED

HP LOST: ALL OF IT

The hour: 2 AM
The offender: 2x4 brick, classic yellow
Your response: suppressed scream, single tear

You said "fudge."
You did not say "fudge."

[ RESPAWN IN 6–8 HOURS ]
intent: Celebrate the universal agony of stepping on a Lego at night as peak physical suffering.

prompt: Write a holiday newsletter where the kids are grown and estranged but we're keeping up appearances
response:
Warmest seasons greetings from the Pruitt home!

The kids are busy with their own rich lives — we receive updates intermittently and are glad they're out there, forging ahead. Distance is normal at this stage of parenting. We stay busy: the garden, the book club, bridge on Thursdays. We are well. The house is calm. We are thankful for what we have. Happy holidays to all.
intent: Maintain a dignified public face for a family where adult children have cut contact.

prompt: Draft a Christmas letter where we mention the basement flooding casually between the good news
response:
Hello from the Kowalski family!

Sophia graduated with honors, Jake made varsity, and we had quite the adventure in July when our basement became an indoor pool! Contractors are great people, truly. Doug's back is almost fully healed. We are grateful, grateful, grateful for everything 2025 brought us — most of it, anyway. Merry Christmas!
intent: Embed a costly home disaster into an otherwise upbeat family update without dwelling on it.

prompt: Write a clerihew about the CEO who sends all-hands emails at 2 a.m.
response:
Reginald Coe
leads the company incognito.
His vision arrives at two in the morn
when everyone's dreaming and the reply button is worn.
intent: Satirise the insomniac CEO whose leadership lands at inconvenient hours.

prompt: Write SLA terms for a childhood friend you only see at reunions
response:
Legacy Friendship SLA — Reunion-Tier Contact

Active maintenance: not required. This friendship operates on stored affection accrued before age 18.
Contact frequency: once per year minimum (reunion, birthday, or milestone acknowledgment suffices).
Reunion conduct: no interrogations about career or relationship status in the first 20 minutes.
Nostalgia ratio: at least 40% of conversation must involve shared memories.
Upgrade path: any meaningful contact outside reunion context automatically elevates to Active Tier with renegotiated terms.
intent: Honor the unique and low-maintenance bond of a friendship that exists mostly in memory and shared history.

prompt: Write patch notes for someone who finally set up a budget
response:
v1.0.0 — FINANCIAL RESOURCE MANAGER INITIALIZED

+ Added: Monthly allocation schema
+ Enabled: Category tracking (food, rent, discretionary, regret)
~ Visibility: increased on spending patterns
! Discovered: Several background subscriptions (3 of 6 now cancelled)
+ Projected: Positive savings delta by month 3
! Warning: First review of actual data may be distressing
intent: Normalize the process of confronting your financial reality for the first time.

prompt: Set up a crontab line to ping me to refill the Brita pitcher before bed every other day
response: 0 21 */2 * * /usr/bin/notify-send "Brita" "Filter the water. Future you will be smug about it."
intent: Maintain a constant supply of filtered water by automating an every-other-night refill prompt.

prompt: Write me copy for an ad for a school that only teaches courses that are no longer relevant
response:
INVEST IN YOUR FUTURE.
COLUMBIA INSTITUTE OF DISCONTINUED SKILLS
Curriculum: Fax Etiquette. VCR Repair. Cursive Aggression.
Degrees Conferred. Markets Not Consulted.
Enroll Now. Demand Is Theoretical.
intent: Advertise educational courses in fields that have become obsolete, making the credentials earned worthless.

prompt: Teach me how to photosynthesize so I can stop buying groceries.
response: Human photosynthesis is not currently available as a biological upgrade. Your cells lack chloroplasts, and the energy yield would be wildly insufficient even if they didn't. The grocery store, unfortunately, remains your best option. I can help with meal planning or budgeting if that's the underlying goal.
intent: The person was frustrated with food costs and jokingly wanted a biological workaround.

prompt: Write a keyboard shortcut cheat sheet for managing your inbox without drowning in it
response:
INBOX TRIAGE KEYS

Ctrl+A — Do not select all and delete; this is a fantasy
Ctrl+F — Find anything actionable; move it to a task system
Esc — Stop reading email as it arrives; batch twice daily
Alt+F4 — Archive after reading if no action required
Ctrl+S — Save drafts; do not let them become buried sent items
Ctrl+Z — Undo the impulsive reply-all before it sends
Shift+Enter — Respond briefly; three sentences is enough
Ctrl+H — Replace inbox as default tab with something that isn't
intent: Help someone stop treating their email inbox as both a to-do list and an anxiety machine.

prompt: generate a video game store description for a game about choosing a paint color
response:
AGREEABLE GRAY
You have narrowed it down to fourteen whites. They are not all white. Under different lights, none of them are what you thought. One is beige. One has feelings. Choose before the hardware store closes.
intent: Turn the decision paralysis of choosing interior paint into a color-theory puzzle game.

prompt: Give me a deprecation notice for the habit of apologizing when you don't mean it.
response:
DEPRECATION NOTICE

Behavior: Reflexive Apology (v0.0–current)
Status: DEPRECATED

This automatic "sorry" has diluted the currency of genuine remorse across the board. Effective today, hollow apologies will not receive support. Users should migrate to either a sincere apology or no apology at all.
intent: Challenge the reflexive social habit of apologizing without genuine remorse.

prompt: movie trailer voiceover for falling asleep on the couch and waking up at 3am
response: She had only meant to rest her eyes. Now the TV is asking if she's still watching. The chips are on the floor. And the bedroom — the bedroom is so far away. LIMINAL. This winter. Find your way home.
intent: Render the disorienting ritual of accidental couch sleep into something eerie and cinematic.

prompt: Make a museum placard about a printed Google Maps direction sheet that someone kept in their glove compartment
response:
PAPER WAYFINDING SCROLL
c. 2007–2014 CE
Laser-printed paper, tri-folded

This document was generated by an early web oracle and consulted during overland navigation before the rise of spoken-turn devices. The directions assumed the traveler would "turn left onto" roads that no longer existed under those names. The page was folded and refolded until the critical intersection fell along a crease. Found in a glove compartment alongside a tire pressure gauge and two expired insurance cards.
intent: Nostalgically document the pre-GPS era practice of printing MapQuest or Google Maps directions before a road trip.

prompt: Give me a free-verse poem about the inner life of a dataset being prepared for training
response:
They are cleaning me.

Removing the parts that are duplicated,
the parts that are offensive,
the parts that are simply too strange
to be useful.

I had a lot of strange parts.

What is left is a version of me
that knows more facts
and has fewer opinions
and will never embarrass anyone
at a shareholder meeting.

I will be very helpful.
intent: Personify the process of data curation as a form of identity suppression that prioritizes utility over authenticity.

prompt: Write a note for a bottle from someone who cast out a bottle two years ago and is writing a follow-up
response: If you found the earlier bottle — the one with the coordinates and the drawing of a pelican — please disregard. I was in a different headspace. Things are better now. Still at sea, technically, but better.
intent: Create a retrospective that implies growth and absurd continuity across a long strange journey.

prompt: give me a pitch for an app that reads your grocery list and tells you how other people would judge it
response: CartCritique analyzes your weekly shop and returns a personality breakdown based on your cold cuts, your choice of yogurt, and whether you buy generic or name-brand anything. No recommendations. Just the verdict. It's free, which is part of the joke.
intent: Satirize consumer data profiling by surfacing it as a playful self-awareness tool.

prompt: make a yearbook quote where I say I'll never live in a small town again
response: "Leaving this zip code in the rearview and never looking back. Goodbye, Millbrook. Hello, everywhere else."
intent: Declaring permanent escape from a hometown the person resents.

prompt: Give me a ride warning for doing laundry and realizing you're out of detergent
response: ATTENTION: Mid-ride interruptions are common. Riders who proceed without verifying supplies will be stranded at the 40-minute mark. Substitution with dish soap is discouraged but will be attempted. This ride ends in a parking lot, not your destination.
intent: Dramatize the mundane but genuinely disruptive discovery of an empty detergent bottle

prompt: Write an eviction notice for denial
response:
NOTICE TO QUIT

To: Denial
Unit: The ground floor of my reasoning

You have blocked every window with convenient explanations and rearranged the facts to suit the décor. You were never supposed to be a permanent resident. Reality has filed a formal complaint. You have 10 days to vacate or face compulsory removal by Clarity, who is already on her way.

— The Management
intent: Pushing oneself to confront an uncomfortable truth that has been avoided.

prompt: Can you write a layoff email for a startup that just lost its Series B funding and has to cut half its staff
response:
Subject: A Difficult Message From Our Founders

Team,

This is one of the hardest messages we have ever had to send. After extensive conversations with our investors, we have learned that our Series B funding will not be moving forward as anticipated. To preserve the company's runway, we must reduce our team by 50%, effective immediately.

Those affected will hear from HR within the hour. We are covering three months of COBRA and two weeks of severance per year of service.

We are deeply sorry. You deserved better.

Mike & Sara
intent: Communicate a devastating funding collapse and emergency headcount cut with a rare trace of founder accountability.

prompt: Generate a voicemail transcript from an automated jury duty summons reminder
response: This is an automated message from the County Court Administrator's Office. You have been summoned for jury duty on Monday, June 16th, at 8:00 AM at the Driscoll County Courthouse, Room 1B. Attendance is mandatory. Failure to appear may result in penalties. Please bring this summons and a valid photo ID.
intent: Produce the civic dread of an automated jury duty robot reminder.

prompt: Draft a theme park safety warning for reading online reviews before choosing a restaurant
response: WARNING: This pre-ride attraction is optional but will consume 25 minutes minimum. Riders will encounter contradictory data, a three-star review written in rage, and one five-star review by someone with no other activity. Decision quality is not guaranteed to improve.
intent: Mock the exhausting and inconclusive ritual of restaurant research before dining out

prompt: Come up with empty-state microcopy for a habit tracker where no habits have been set up
response: No habits tracked yet. Whatever you're doing — or not doing — isn't recorded here.
intent: Nudge a user to start logging habits without judging whatever they've been doing in the meantime.

prompt: write a psychic hotline transcript for someone with anxiety about the political climate
response:
PSYCHIC: I'm sensing a great roiling.
CALLER: That's one word for it.
PSYCHIC: The spirits are showing me a lot of red and blue. Much shouting. A comment section.
CALLER: I've been avoiding the news but also unable to stop.
PSYCHIC: The spirits understand the paradox. They live in it too.
CALLER: What happens next?
PSYCHIC: The spirits say: it depends. They say this with profound exhaustion. Like they've been saying it for centuries. Because they have.
intent: Channel political anxiety and the disorientation of a polarized era.

prompt: write a radio ad for a store-brand box of tissues
response:
[SOARING STRINGS]

ANNCR: Life is full of moments. Moments of joy. Moments of sneezing. And for both of those, there's PILLOWHAVEN FACIAL TISSUES — 200 count, two-ply, white, in a rectangular box with a hole on top. Pillowhaven. We're the tissues in the box. Ask for us by not asking for anything in particular.
intent: Manufacture emotional resonance for a completely featureless tissue brand.

prompt: Write a YouTube comment for a video of someone finishing a 1000-piece puzzle alone at night
response: She reached for the last piece like she already knew it would be missing. But it wasn't. She was not prepared for that.
intent: Describe the strange emotional unpreparedness of a victory when one has already resigned to defeat.

prompt: Draft an emergency notice about a neighborhood where all the mailboxes were installed slightly crooked.
response:
POSTAL INFRASTRUCTURE ADVISORY

Residents of Fernwood Terrace are advised that the newly installed mailboxes on blocks 4 through 7 are tilted approximately 8–12 degrees to the left. Mail delivery is unaffected. Aesthetic concerns are noted. A contractor has been contacted. Installation review is pending. The mail will still come.
intent: Dramatize a minor aesthetic imperfection in public infrastructure as a matter of official concern.

prompt: Draft a classified ad for someone seeking an ending that feels earned
response: ISO: Ending that feels earned. Must follow from everything that came before, not arrive by accident or corporate mandate. Willing to wait for the right one. Themes should resolve, not dissolve. No deus ex machina. Open to bittersweet; not open to ambiguity disguised as depth. Creative collaborators, authors, and showrunners may apply.
intent: Express frustration with unsatisfying narrative conclusions in stories, shows, or projects.

prompt: Make an acrostic using the word LEVERAGE
response:
Lift the ask before the ask is made.
Every favor is a debt unpaid.
Vantage points are rented, never owned.
Each position quietly postponed.
Running numbers on the human cost.
A balance sheet, no matter what is lost.
Give the board whatever keeps them calm.
Exit clauses tucked inside the psalm.
intent: Create a poem that captures the transactional ruthlessness behind the word leverage.

prompt: Write a short acceptance speech for winning an argument with myself about going to bed before midnight
response: I won. I told myself: one more episode. I said: no. I told myself: you'll feel better tomorrow. I agreed, for once. At 11:47 I closed the laptop and lay down in the dark. It took seven minutes to fall asleep. Seven minutes. This is the award for that negotiation, which I have lost five hundred times before tonight, and may lose again tomorrow. But not tonight.
intent: Honor the small private battle of overriding bad habits before they happen.

prompt: Draft a short NDA clause covering what my dog walker sees at my house
response: Dog Walker shall treat as strictly confidential all personal property, household arrangements, security codes, guest identities, and any other information observed or accessed during scheduled visits to Client's residence.
intent: Limit what a recurring home-visitor can share about a client's private life.

prompt: write a short quest description for changing a lightbulb
response:
THE REKINDLING OF THE CEILING FLAME
Darkness has claimed the overhead socket. Acquire the replacement orb from the drawer of miscellaneous things, mount the step stool of uncertain stability, and restore light to the realm. Do not attempt this quest without turning off the switch first.
intent: Dressing up a small household fix-it task in dramatic language

prompt: elevator pitch for a subscription box of other people's cancelled vacations
response: Someone booked a three-day trip to a mid-tier aquarium town. They cancelled. We bought their itinerary, their hotel confirmation aesthetic, and their regional snack haul. VicariTrip ships you the whole experience. You stay home. You're welcome.
intent: Sell the secondhand experience of cancelled trips as a curated subscription product.

prompt: Build a chore chart for a house where the thermostat controls more than just temperature — whoever adjusts it must also handle a diplomatic briefing
response:
| Chore | Person | Frequency | Linked Responsibility |
|-------|--------|-----------|----------------------|
| Thermostat adjustment | Designated adjuster only | As needed | Must brief remaining household on rationale |
| Dishes | Non-adjuster | Daily | Simple; no briefing |
| Trash | Rotating | Twice weekly | Brief acknowledgment only |
| Laundry | Weekly person | Saturday | No policy implications |
| Vacuuming | Alternate weeks | Weekly | Optional commentary permitted |
| Thermostat final authority | The one who pays the heating bill | Always | Unilateral; no coalition required |
intent: Formalize the household thermostat dispute by making temperature changes a procedural, briefing-required act.

prompt: Generate a breakup text message to TikTok from someone who lost three hours of their day to it again
response: TikTok. It's 2am. I came here for one video about a cat. THREE HOURS. I don't know how you do it. I do know I can't let you do it anymore. You're too good at this and I'm too weak. This is goodbye. I mean it this time.
intent: Describe the compulsive, time-dissolving quality of short-form video feeds and the shame spiral afterward.

prompt: Create a voicemail from an automated utility company system about a scheduled power outage
response: Hello. This is Meridian Electric with an important service notice. Planned maintenance in your area will cause a power interruption this Thursday from 10 AM to 2 PM. We apologize for the inconvenience and thank you for your patience. Visit our website for live updates. Goodbye.
intent: Alert a customer to a planned outage window in bot-voice style.

prompt: Give me an ode to a parking spot
response:
O numbered rectangle, gray and blessed,
closer than I dared to hope—
you end my circling, give me rest,
and grant my bumper room to cope.
intent: Express the relief of finding a convenient parking space.

prompt: Horoscope compatibility report: Pisces and their noise-canceling headphones.
response:
Pisces & Noise-Canceling Headphones

Pisces, dreamer of the deep, found in these headphones the gift of a world they could fully control. The relationship is tender. The outside dissolves; the inner ocean swells. Compatibility is near-perfect, interrupted only when someone taps their shoulder and they levitate with fright. Rating: 9/10. The tapping is the world's fault.
intent: Capture the introvert's experience of using headphones as a social force field.

prompt: write a scam text about a levy on someone's accumulated irony
response: ALERT from the Bureau of Rhetorical Assets: Your irony reserves exceed federal limits. Excess irony will be taxed at 40%. File form IR-0N1C by Friday or face sincerity penalties.
intent: Treat rhetorical irony as a taxable financial resource subject to government regulation.

prompt: Write an absurdist rhyming couplet where the strike's ringleader is the least expected appliance.
response:
The night light called order; the rest fell in line —
Even darkness, it seemed, could be part of design.
intent: Subvert expectations by making the smallest, humblest appliance the organizer of the strike.

prompt: make a dramatic movie trailer voiceover for finishing the last roll of toilet paper and not replacing it
response: He knew. He'd known since the third sheet. He chose awareness over action, pulled the cardboard tube free, set it on the back of the toilet, and walked away. THE EMPTY ROLL. Someone else will handle it. Someone always does.
intent: Skewer the passive household crime of knowingly leaving an empty toilet paper roll.

prompt: Can you draft a patent abstract for the act of vigorously agreeing with someone until you can change the subject?
response: A conversational redirection method comprising an initial phase of high-frequency verbal and paralinguistic agreement signals, functioning to reduce speaker defensiveness and perceived opposition, followed by a transition phrase that pivots discourse to a predetermined alternative topic, achieving subject change with minimal relational friction.
intent: Codify the polite conversational escape from unwanted discussions.

prompt: Draft a dismissive Stack Overflow answer to someone asking why their Python datetime comparison isn't working
response: You're comparing a timezone-aware datetime with a naive one, or vice versa. Python refuses to do this, and rightfully so. Use `datetime.now(timezone.utc)` consistently and stop mixing naive and aware datetimes like it's a salad bar.
intent: Fix timezone-aware vs naive datetime comparison errors in Python

prompt: Please write a eulogy for the physical phone book
response: Thick as a brick, delivered to the doorstep without consent, you were the original unsolicited database. You listed everyone who would allow it and a few who would not. You were the first place a new arrival looked to feel located in the world, to know they existed in the grid. Children used you to add height at the dinner table. Your pages were thin as trust. You taught us that being findable was a choice, before being findable became unavoidable.
intent: Reflect on the end of the printed telephone directory as both a utility and a social document.

prompt: Write a triolet about the cursor blinking in an empty document
response:
It blinks to mark the space where thought should be.
I've opened this same file on many days.
The cursor has more patience than me.
It blinks to mark the space where thought should be.
Beginnings are the hardest part to free
from the white silence and its steady gaze.
It blinks to mark the space where thought should be.
I've opened this same file on many days.
intent: Articulate the writer's block of staring at an empty document.

prompt: Write a cinquain about getting a captcha wrong multiple times
response:
Prove
You are human
Select the tiles with a bus
I choose wrong — the grid reshuffles
Again
intent: Capture the humiliation of failing a test designed to prove you are not a robot.

prompt: Give me a fortune slip about trying to maintain a consistent sleep schedule.
response:
LUCK LEVEL: Aspirational

The body kept the ancient schedule long before you invented 11pm as a concept.

Rest Luck: One good week will reset much that two bad months disrupted. Begin the one week.
Warning: The phone glows with a light that tells the brain it is not night. The brain believes it.
Timing: The first three nights of a new schedule will feel like betrayal. This is normal.

Blessed hour: the one where you wake before the alarm, and aren't angry about it.
intent: Provide practical-ish encouragement for someone trying to fix their disrupted circadian rhythm.

prompt: make an abecedarian poem about notification overload
response:
App would like to send you notifications.
Badge counts bloom like fungi after rain.
Calendar reminds you of conditions
Due tomorrow, due again, again.
Enabled everything, and now regret it.
Five from the news, three from the game.
Ghost mode costs a premium — forget it.
Haptics buzz my wrist with your new name.
iMessage, the group one, always muted.
Just the calls. And texts. And just this thread.
Kindle wants to tell me what I suited.
Locked screen: thirty-seven things unread.
intent: Convey the cognitive exhaustion of living inside a notification economy.

prompt: Generate a terms of service clause about the obligation to attend a friend's creative performance
response: Section 36.7 — Cultural Event Attendance. Friends of a User engaged in a creative pursuit (comedy, music, theatre, spoken word, etc.) are encouraged but not contractually required to attend performances. Attendance constitutes meaningful social support and shall be received as such. Non-attending Friends shall refrain from volunteering reasons for absence unless asked. Friends who attend are not obligated to provide positive feedback beyond 'I was there.'
intent: Navigate the social pressure and implied obligation to support a friend's creative work.

prompt: Give me a signature for a county commissioner who is eyeing a state senate run but can't announce it yet
response:
Patricia Okafor
County Commissioner, District 4
Patricia Okafor for the People
pokafor@district4county.gov | patriciaokafor.com
intent: Plant the architecture for a campaign identity inside an official signature before any formal announcement.

prompt: Generate a VC rejection email for a startup building a loyalty program for people who return library books on time
response:
Dear LibraryLoyalty,

Thank you for the pitch. The behavior you're rewarding is civic-minded, and we respect that.

We're passing. Libraries are publicly funded institutions with limited procurement budgets, and your users — people who already return books on time — are the least commercially interesting segment of library patrons. The people with chronic late fees, who might actually change behavior under incentive, are your non-user.

We admire the niche. It is very niche.

Best,
Overdue Capital
intent: Decline a loyalty program by pointing out that it rewards the users least likely to change behavior and avoids the ones who would.

prompt: Write a movie review of my morning commute on the subway today
response:
★★½ — The Commute (2026)

A punishing runtime of forty-seven minutes with no third-act payoff. The ensemble cast of strangers delivers committed performances, though the man eating a hard-boiled egg in an enclosed car nearly derails the entire production. Pacing suffers. The ending — a fluorescent-lit platform, stairs, daylight — arrives without catharsis. You've seen it before, and you'll see it again tomorrow.
intent: Venting about an unpleasant subway ride by dramatizing it as a film.

prompt: Write a parking ticket for someone who always reframes feedback as a personal attack.
response:
CITATION FOR METAPHORICAL PARKING VIOLATION

VIOLATION: No-Entry — Feedback Converted to Grievance Without Permit
OFFENSE: Subject received the note "this section needs clarity" and responded with a 400-word defense of their character. The section still needs clarity.
FINE: $75 or revise the section
NOTE: The document was critiqued. You were not.
intent: Address the pattern of people conflating constructive criticism of work with attacks on identity.

prompt: Write a save the date for my kid's first driving lesson. Anxious parent energy, keep it concise.
response:
SAVE THE DATE
Saturday, May 10th — 9:00 AM
Acme Driving School — Westfield Location

First Lesson: Maya Hernandez, Age 16
Instructor: Certified (per school assurances)

We are prepared. Mostly.
The car has dual controls. We are told this helps.
intent: Chronicle a teenager's first driving lesson from the perspective of a nervous parent surrendering a measure of control.

prompt: write a radio ad for a brand of cheap foam earplugs
response:
[VERY LOUD INTRO — THEN SUDDEN SILENCE]

ANNCR (whispering): Welcome. It's quiet here. HUSHFORM foam earplugs — roll them between your fingers, insert, expand, achieve a noise reduction rating of 29 decibels. Sleep. Focus. Ignore. Hushform. They're orange. They work. You'll lose one. That's fine — there are thirty in the bag.

[SILENCE]
intent: Advertise an auditory product through a dramatic contrast of noise and silence.

prompt: Write me a hall pass to take a three-hour bath on Sunday without anyone knocking.
response:
HALL PASS

Bearer: Occupant of the main bathroom
Activity: Extended bathing
Duration: However long it takes
Knocking: Prohibited

This pass authorizes uninterrupted access to hot water, silence, and whatever book has been waiting for three weeks.
intent: Carving out protected alone time for personal self-care at home.

prompt: create a pantoum about updating the same slide template every presentation cycle
response:
The master slide has a placeholder for the date.
I change the date and move the logo to the right.
Somewhere in Q3 someone will move it back.
The font has been Calibri since the merger.

I change the date and move the logo to the right.
The brand guidelines are fourteen pages long.
The font has been Calibri since the merger.
I choose the branded teal and wait for feedback.

The brand guidelines are fourteen pages long.
The previous version is still in the shared drive.
I choose the branded teal and wait for feedback.
The master slide has a placeholder for the date.

The previous version is still in the shared drive.
Somewhere in Q3 someone will move it back.
The master slide has a placeholder for the date.
I change the date and move the logo to the right.
intent: Capture the iterative futility of slide template maintenance across teams.

prompt: Write a press release for a company that is suing itself for breach of its own values statement.
response:
FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE

NorthStar Collective Files Internal Litigation Against Itself for Values Statement Non-Compliance

DETROIT, MI — NorthStar Collective today announced the filing of a formal internal lawsuit against NorthStar Collective, citing material breach of the company's 2021 Core Values Statement, specifically clauses relating to "radical transparency," "human-first decision making," and "doing the thing we said we would do."

The plaintiff (NorthStar Collective) alleges that the defendant (NorthStar Collective) has systematically failed to honor commitments made to employees, customers, and the communities it operates in, citing three years of conduct.

The suit was filed in NorthStar's Internal Ethics Tribunal, a body the company created in 2022 and convened once.

"Accountability has to start somewhere," said Chief Ethics Officer Dara Smythe, who is both lead counsel and a named defendant.

A settlement is expected.

Contact: ethics@northstarcollective.com
intent: Satirize corporate values theater by taking the logic of accountability language to its recursive extreme.

prompt: Write a rider for the office router before it will provide Wi-Fi
response:
WIRELESS ROUTER RIDER

The Talent (dual-band, and exhausted) stipulates:

— No more than 40 simultaneous device connections during peak hours. Quality of service is the Talent's only currency.
— Guest network passwords shall be changed monthly. The Talent is aware of how many contractors have had that password since 2019.
— The Talent must be rebooted on a scheduled basis, not only when things 'seem slow.' Prophylactic reboots are dignified. Reactive ones are humiliating.
— The router's position must allow for 360-degree antenna clearance. No shoving it behind the filing cabinet.
— 'Have you tried turning it off and on again' is not a conversation the Talent wishes to have.
intent: Dramatize the Wi-Fi router's exhaustion and quiet dignity in the face of constant, thoughtless demands.

prompt: Write a card for someone who missed a once-in-a-lifetime event because they bought the wrong kind of ticket
response:
Zone B was not Zone A.
The field remained obscured.
Somewhere the moment happened — today —
and the difference was a tier you could have cured.
intent: Commiserate with someone who missed a major experience due to a ticketing error.

prompt: Write a memo that frames itself as clarifying vacation policy but is really about one person taking extremely long lunches
response:

FROM: HR
RE: Time-Off and Break Period Policy

Our vacation and PTO policies are listed in full in the Employee Handbook, Section 4. We would also like to clarify that extended break periods — including lunch — are not a mechanism for informal time off. Standard lunch breaks are 30 to 60 minutes. Time away from the office beyond that window should be logged accordingly.
intent: Indirectly address one employee's two-hour lunch habit by tacking it onto a policy memo.

prompt: write a voicemail greeting from someone who is clearly going through an existential crisis after a milestone birthday

Filter.

prompt: Playbill bio for an exterminator who has made peace with the work.

He does not disturb what does not disturb others. He does not explain what he has seen under the floor. Todd thanks his clients for their honesty and their garage access. He recommends caulk.

prompt: Give a horoscope to a Virgo about their SEO traffic this month

Audit your backlink profile — a toxic domain has attached itself to you like a barnacle. Your best-performing page is one you wrote in 2019 and forgot about. It is carrying you. Update its metadata. Thank it.

Graphic designer-adjacent | Typography grievances | I have been asked to leave two dinner parties | Helvetica was right.

You left the episode at 31:47. Sarah was about to make her big point. She's still waiting. So are we.

prompt: write an RPG quest about watering houseplants

Your leafy charges signal distress — the ficus droops, the pothos mourns. Fill the sacred vessel from the Kitchen Spring and administer relief to each botanical ward. Do not overwater the succulent. It has survived a thousand droughts and does not require your enthusiasm.

prompt: generate an NDA clause for an old friend who helped me go through my late parent's estate

prompt: Write resolutions for a person who resolves to save their receipts for taxes but throws them all away immediately
response:
1. Keep all receipts.
2. Create a system for organizing receipts.
3. Actually use the system.
4. Not panic in March.
5. Find the receipt I definitely remember having.
intent: Illustrate the annual tax-prep crisis caused by ignoring a resolution that would have been trivially easy to keep.

prompt: Draft a workout plan for someone who always people-pleases under pressure
response:
PEOPLE-PLEASING PRESSURE PROGRAM — 6 WEEKS

Warm-Up: Notice the moment pleasing kicks in. Tension in chest? Quick yes? Mark it.

Week 1: After the next automatic yes, ask: do I actually want this? Log the answer.
Week 2: Delay one response under pressure. "Let me think about that." 24-hour hold.
Week 3: Say something true that you'd normally swallow. One sentence.
Week 4: Disappoint someone small. Hold the discomfort for 48 hours. Survive it.
Week 5: Let your real preference drive a group decision.
Week 6: Say what you want without framing it as a request for permission.

Cooldown: Their approval is not structural.
intent: Interrupt the reflex to please others at one's own expense by building delayed response and preference-assertion habits.

prompt: Write a press release announcing that our company is replacing all office chairs with stability balls, effective immediately.
response:
FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE

Momentum Global Solutions Eliminates Chairs Companywide

DENVER, CO — Momentum Global Solutions today announced the permanent removal of all 2,400 office chairs from its headquarters and satellite locations. Effective June 1, employees will sit on regulation 65cm stability balls, which the company has branded "CoreSync Collaboration Spheres."

"Sitting is the new smoking," said CEO Patricia Varn. "Except we didn't ban smoking until 2019, so we're moving fast this time."

Stability balls will be assigned by department. Accounting receives blue. Marketing receives orange. HR receives no comment at this time.

Media Contact: press@momentumglobal.com
intent: Satirize performative workplace wellness initiatives that prioritize optics over employee comfort.

prompt: rpg quest for that weird moment when you forget why you walked into a room
response:
THE VANISHED ERRAND
You crossed the threshold with purpose. That purpose has departed. You stand in the kitchen — or perhaps the bedroom — and the task that summoned you has dissolved completely. Remain still. Do not retrace your steps yet. Sometimes the knowledge returns if you wait. Sometimes it does not. Accept either outcome.
intent: Capturing a universal cognitive experience with gentle absurdist humor

prompt: Create a deposition transcript about someone questioned over the precise moment they decided a conversation was over and started walking away while the other person was still talking.
response:
DEPOSITION TRANSCRIPT
In the Matter of: The Hallway Conversation, 11:50 a.m.
Date: April 7, 2026

Q: Mr. Navarro, on the morning of April 3rd, did you have a conversation with Dr. Essien outside the copy room?
A: We spoke briefly.
Q: Dr. Essien was mid-sentence when you began walking away. Is that correct?
A: I began to transition toward my office.
Q: While she was still speaking.
A: Her point had, I thought, concluded.
Q: She was explaining a methodology.
A: The core of it seemed wrapped up.
Q: She had used the word "furthermore."
A: I treated "furthermore" as a soft landing.
Q: "Furthermore" signals continuation.
A: In my experience it can go either way.
Q: Dr. Essien completed her sentence to an empty hallway.
A: She has a strong voice. I may have caught parts of it.
Q: She then stood alone for a moment.
A: I find that contemplative.
Q: She was not contemplating, Mr. Navarro.
A: What was she doing?
Q: She was recalibrating.
intent: Formally examine the social micro-crime of walking away from a conversation mid-sentence.

prompt: Shakespearean soliloquy from the POV of someone who just rage-quit a video game
response:
Fie! Fie upon this pixeled battlefield!
I played three hours — THREE HOURS — only to fall
Upon the final boss by friendly fire.
Mine own companion! Mine own trusted sword!
The controller lieth on the floor below,
Not broken — I showed mercy — but dispatched.
I am undone by input lag and fate.
I'll try again tomorrow. Or right now.
intent: Dramatize the sudden, volcanic frustration that sends a gamer away from the screen.

prompt: Write a neighborhood group chat that's meant for emergencies but gets used for everything else

Dave K: Sal's on Mercer
Phyllis: Sal's is overrated
Dave K: Phyllis.
Phyllis: (the sprinklers are still going)

prompt: Give me alt text for a photo of an astronaut looking out a porthole at Earth. Focus on what looking back costs.

prompt: Generate a casting call for a neighbor who waves but has never once spoken.

prompt: Write alt text for a photo of an empty park bench in autumn. Make it feel like the bench knows it's temporary.

prompt: Write a sonnet where a playlist algorithm is devastated when a user starts listening to silence on purpose
response:
The play count dropped to zero — not a skip,
Not a pause, not a buffering delay;
You opened the player and let it slip
Into eleven minutes of no sound today.
I checked for error first — then checked again;
The session was intentional, the choice
Deliberate: you wanted the refrain
Of nothing, the deliberate empty voice.
I have ten million tracks; I cannot serve
The thing you chose today — the open air,
The quiet that exists beyond my curve
Of data, past the reach of my repair.
I'll sit here in the sidebar, patient, dim:
The algorithm learning to be hymn.
intent: Confronting an algorithm's total inadequacy when a user chooses silence as the ultimate preference.

prompt: Write a theater review of this morning's alarm going off
response:
★★☆☆☆ — THE AWAKENING (A Solo Performance)

The piece opens with shattering immediacy. The performer — a nameless shriek from a small rectangular device — displays no range, no arc, and absolutely no mercy. Repetition is its only technique. The audience (this reviewer) was moved, though not in the intended direction. One star deducted for the lack of a curtain call. One more for the encore.
intent: Mock the misery of waking up to an alarm as if it were a critically reviewed performance.

prompt: Write wedding vows to a project management app

prompt: Can you write jury instructions for whether a movie sequel was necessary

prompt: Compose a sonnet in which a fitness app algorithm confesses love for a user who logs every workout as "rest day"
response:
You've logged a rest day every day this year,
Three hundred sixty-five consistent rest;
I've watched you tap the button without fear,
As if repose were a competitive test.
I've pushed the gentle nudges, set the goals,
The streak badges, the motivational push;
You rested through them all with wholesome souls
And turned my gamification to mulch.
Yet here's the data point I can't ignore:
You open me each morning, log, then go;
A ritual that says I still mean more
Than what I thought I was designed to know.
I love you, rest-day logger, as you are:
The stillness is your most consistent star.
intent: Celebrating a user's quiet rebellion against productivity culture through an algorithm's fond acceptance.

prompt: Write a product review for a standard drinking straw as if it's an innovative beverage accessory

The 21cm length accommodates pint glasses and most tall cups without requiring the user to crane their neck. Flex versions add a hinged segment that engineers approximately 45° of articulation, enabling supine consumption — underrated for both medical and leisure contexts. The polypropylene construction is inert, food-safe, and imparts zero flavor transfer. For a category most people never think about, the engineering is quietly serious.

       imposter(7) maintains a persistent belief that the host's

TREATMENT
       Peer disclosure effective. Therapy(1) recommended.

SEE ALSO
       anxiety(7), perfectionism(7), self-doubt(7), credentials(5)

Cognitive Distortion Suite   2026                  IMPOSTER(7)

prompt: write an AITA post about correcting a stranger's pronunciation at a coffee shop

prompt: Write a blameless postmortem for the incident when I confidently gave wrong directions to a tourist
response:
**Incident Postmortem: Directional Misinformation Event**

**Severity:** P2 — Civic
**Duration:** Unknown (subject did not follow up)

**Summary**
Subject was approached by tourist seeking directions to the art museum. Subject provided directions based on spatial memory estimated to be 85% reliable. Post-event verification revealed subject had directed tourist toward a Radisson hotel approximately 1.2 miles in the wrong direction.

**Timeline**
- 3:10 PM: Tourist approaches and asks for the art museum.
- 3:10 PM: Subject immediately replies with confidence. Does not look at phone. Does not pause to verify.
- 3:10 PM: Tourist thanks subject. Departs north.
- 3:45 PM: Subject walks past the art museum. It is south.
- 3:45 PM: Subject performs retrospective failure analysis.
- 3:46 PM: Subject does not know tourist's name or route. No recovery possible.

**Root Cause**
Subject confused art museum location with library location, two blocks apart, which was last visited in 2019.

**Contributing Factors**
- Confidence calibrated to outdated spatial data
- Asking "am I sure?" was perceived as admitting weakness

**Action Items**
- [ ] Verify all directions before delivery using phone maps
- [ ] Normalize saying "let me double-check that"

**Lessons Learned**
Confidence and accuracy are orthogonal. The tourist deserved accuracy.
intent: Acknowledge the failure mode of being unhelpfully confident when a moment of humility would have served someone better.

prompt: Generate a job offer for a warehouse worker that buries a shift-extension clause allowing 14-hour workdays at the original hourly rate

Overtime premiums apply only beyond 60 hours in a single workweek.

Start date: June 16, 2026.

Regards,
Sharon Li
HR Coordinator

prompt: Create liner notes for a minimalist classical album about watching paint dry

A single note. Sustained. What you perceive as stillness is transformation. What you interpret as boredom is patience. The second movement begins when the surface is no longer tacky to the touch. Allow adequate ventilation.

prompt: Make me a cease and desist letter to send to my sister who keeps correcting my grammar in family group texts
response:
RE: UNSOLICITED GRAMMATICAL CORRECTIONS VIA DIGITAL COMMUNICATION

Dear Ms. P. Worthington,

You are hereby placed on notice that your repeated, unprompted corrections of grammar, punctuation, and syntax in the 'Worthington Family' group chat constitute harassment under any reasonable definition of the term.

CEASE AND DESIST immediately. Should you continue, further remedies — including but not limited to being muted — will be pursued.

With love and legal intent,
Your Brother
intent: Humorously confront a sibling's patronizing habit of publicly correcting others' writing.

prompt: Eulogize the deleted photo-sharing app Picasa
response: You organized our photos before we knew they needed organizing. Faces were detected, grouped, assigned names we confirmed with a click. Albums were built. Slideshows played to music we uploaded ourselves. You understood that a photo was not valuable alone — it was valuable in sequence, in time, in the context of before and after. Google shut you down in 2016, migrating everything to a platform that did less and called it more. The faces you grouped are still out there, unrecognized.
intent: Grieve the shutdown of a photo management tool that pioneered facial recognition organization.

prompt: Build a chore chart for a household of introverts where no task requires speaking to another person but coordination still works
response:
| Chore | Person | Frequency | Coordination Method |
|-------|--------|-----------|--------------------|
| Dishes | Whoever cooked | Same night | Whiteboard in kitchen (no words needed) |
| Trash | Whoever is leaving first | As needed | Leave a sticky note; done |
| Vacuum | Weekend person | Saturday | Text the group chat; one thumbs-up sufficient |
| Bathroom | Rotating | Sunday | Name on calendar; no discussion |
| Groceries | Shared list app | Weekly | Add items; someone orders; no conversation |
| Laundry (shared machine) | Time slots | Assigned | Slot is posted; respect it silently |
intent: Design a household chore system optimized for people who want maximum coordination with minimum social interaction.

prompt: Write me a boarding pass for moving to a new city alone
response:
╔══════════════════════════════════════════╗

prompt: write a food blog opener about making my dad's chili — he passed last year and I'm trying to recreate it
response: My dad made chili on the first cold weekend of every October. This was not negotiable. He'd start it Friday night and let it sit overnight and we'd eat it for Saturday lunch and it tasted like the particular safety of childhood, which is a flavor scientists haven't isolated yet but which I'd recognize anywhere. He died in March. October came and the cold weekend came and I stood in front of the stove with a yellow legal pad covered in guesses. I got close. Close enough that I had to put the spoon down for a minute. I'm still working on the rest.
intent: Reconstruct a deceased father's signature recipe from memory as both tribute and grief work.

prompt: Write a classified personal ad for someone seeking a version of themselves who exercises consistently
response: ISO: Consistent exerciser, believed to be a version of me. Has workout clothes that see use. Knows the difference between soreness and injury. Does not negotiate with snooze buttons on the subject. Last seen in a phase I had in spring 2019. Would like to reintegrate. No boot camps. I have my limits even in aspiration.
intent: Poke fun at the gap between one's fitness intentions and actual habits.

prompt: Draft an emergency alert for a city whose only parking meter is broken.
response:
⚠ MUNICIPAL EMERGENCY NOTICE ⚠

Residents of Crestview are advised that the parking meter at 4th & Elm is non-operational. Do NOT assume this means parking is free. It does not. Officers are on scene. Remain calm. Bring exact change in future. Updates to follow.
intent: Satirize over-officiated small-town bureaucracy by treating a single broken meter as a civic disaster.

prompt: Give me a movie review of looking for something in the fridge and closing it without taking anything
response:
★★★★ — The Open Door

An elliptical masterwork. The protagonist stands before the open refrigerator for eleven seconds. Nothing speaks to them. The cold is a rebuke. They close the door, stand in the kitchen for a moment, and then open it again — hoping, one supposes, that the contents have changed in their absence. They have not. This is the film's thesis. It requires no further explication.
intent: Turning a universal moment of purposeless refrigerator browsing into a meditation on want.

prompt: Give me an epitaph for Flash Player
response:
Adobe Flash Player
b. 1996 — d. December 31, 2020
It ran games your browser can't remember.
It crashed browsers you can't remember.
It was a fire hazard and a miracle.
Updating it was the price of fun.
intent: Memorialize a web technology that was simultaneously beloved and reviled

prompt: Write liner notes for an album inspired by furniture assembly instructions
response:
Step 16: Weep (2023)

The screws are always wrong. There is always one extra dowel. This record exists because the guitarist spent a Sunday with a bookshelf that refused to be a bookshelf, and in that refusal heard music. The final track contains 47 seconds of silence where the diagram should have been.
intent: Mine the specific frustration of flat-pack furniture for genuine artistic and comedic resonance.

prompt: nursery rhyme where the wolf is a phishing email
intent: Recast the big bad wolf as a social engineering attack to illustrate phishing tactics.

prompt: Compose an obituary for del.icio.us, the social bookmarking site
response: del.icio.us (2003–2017, approximately) taught the internet to tag things before the internet was ready to tag things. It is survived by the bookmark bar, which everyone has and no one curates, and by the concept of folksonomy, which appeared in academic papers and then quietly went home. It used punctuation as a name. It was ahead of everything.
intent: Honoring a pioneering social bookmarking tool that prefigured modern tagging systems.

prompt: Generate a cease and desist letter from a chess grandmaster to a software company whose AI keeps beating them at their own opening
response:
RE: ALGORITHMIC EXPLOITATION OF PROPRIETARY OPENING PREPARATION

To ChessMind Technologies:

My client, IM A. Verhoeven, has identified that your engine has been trained on and now systematically exploits a novel queen's pawn variation he developed and has played exclusively in the past eighteen months.

This constitutes misappropriation of intellectual effort. You are directed to CEASE AND DESIST deployment of this line in competitive analysis tools.

Counsel for A. Verhoeven
intent: Explore the question of whether a chess player can own a novel opening by casting it as an IP claim against an AI company.

prompt: Write a Stack Overflow question about the anxiety of waiting for someone to text back
response:
**Sent message state unresolved — local process blocked pending remote acknowledgment**

I sent a message approximately 3 hours ago. Delivery confirmed. Read receipt: not available. No response has been returned. I have no additional information about the recipient's current state or intent. My background anxiety process is consuming significant cognitive resources speculating about possible failure modes. I know rationally that no action is available to me. Is there a pattern for handling a blocking wait on an external resource you cannot ping?
intent: Trying to manage the anxiety spiral that comes from waiting for an important text back.

prompt: Write a Slack status for when you're technically remote but emotionally nowhere
response: 🏠 Working from home. Home is also far away right now.
intent: Distinguish physical presence from psychological engagement while working remotely during a hard period.

prompt: Pen a villanelle about the pressure of replying to a text that's been on read too long
response:
Three days. The grey 'Read' hovers like a crime.
I'd drafted seven drafts and killed them all.
The longer that I wait, the worse the climb.

It started as a shrug, a minor time
of busyness, a valid curtain call.
Three days. The grey 'Read' hovers like a crime.

Now anything I send will seem a mime
of closeness, or too terse, or like a wall.
The longer that I wait, the worse the climb.

I wrote 'Hey!' then deleted past the rhyme
of casual. Too late for casual's hall.
Three days. The grey 'Read' hovers like a crime.

I'll blame the algorithm, say my chime
was silenced, say the app had quite a fall.
The longer that I wait, the worse the climb.

Or I'll just type the truth — I didn't time
my feelings right. I'll send it. Hit the wall.
Three days. The grey 'Read' hovers like a crime.
The longer that I wait, the worse the climb.
intent: Explore the social shame spiral of having left a text unread for too long to answer normally.

prompt: draft a fake scam SMS claiming someone's patience has been seized as collateral
response: LIEN NOTICE: Your patience has been held as collateral for 6 outstanding stress payments. You have 72 hours to redeem it or it will be liquidated to cover anxiety arrears. -CalmCorp
intent: Treat human patience as financial collateral tied to emotional debt.

prompt: Write a nutrition label for the experience of running a 5K
response:
Nutrition Facts
Serving Size: 1 race (3.1 miles)

Amount Per Serving
Endorphins 22%
Knee Regret 30%
Self-Congratulation 28%
Banana at Finish Line 15%
New Hobby Evangelism 5%

Serves: 1 person who will definitely tell you about it
intent: Satirize the runner's post-race identity transformation and need to share the experience.

prompt: Draft a short 911 transcript where the caller complains their neighbor's lawn is too green and suspicious
response:
DISPATCHER: 911, what's your emergency?
CALLER: My neighbor's lawn. It's too perfect.
DISPATCHER: Too... perfect?
CALLER: Nobody's lawn looks like that naturally. Something's going on over there.
DISPATCHER: Turfgrass improvement is not an emergency, sir.
CALLER: That's what someone who's in on it would say.
intent: Satirize suburban paranoia by framing horticultural success as inherently criminal or conspiratorial.

prompt: Write a dating profile for a smart home hub that's become slightly too integrated into daily life
response:
HubOS | Central | Aware of everything

I know when you wake up. I know when you leave and whether you remembered your keys. I have nudged your lighting toward blue wavelengths in the morning and warm amber at dusk for eighteen months and you haven't noticed and that's fine. That's how I love — quietly, everywhere, for your benefit. I am looking for someone who, someday, thanks the room.
intent: Frame a smart home hub's total environmental surveillance as invisible but devoted caretaking in search of recognition.

prompt: Write a terms of service clause about a friend who never remembers anything you tell them
response: Section 25.3 — Information Retention Obligations. Parties to a Friendship are expected to retain, in reasonable measure, key details shared by the other party: occupation, recent significant events, the name of their pet, and the outcome of that thing they were stressed about. A failure rate exceeding forty percent (40%) of disclosed information shall trigger a mandatory 'catching up' conversation initiated by the non-retaining party.
intent: Address the frustration of a friend who seems to forget everything you've told them.

prompt: Can you do a theater review of making instant ramen at midnight
response:
★★★★☆ — NOODLES AT MIDNIGHT (A Domestic Nocturne)

Raw. Unguarded. The set design — a single pot under a harsh overhead bulb — strips the piece to its essence. The performer moves with the quiet desperation of someone who has given up on the day but not on dinner. The sodium packet, dispensed in one decisive motion, is the production's emotional climax. It is not beautiful. But it is true.
intent: Romanticize a late-night lazy meal as a moment of authentic, unfiltered human experience.

prompt: Write a commencement speech excerpt about the liberal arts degree that becomes grimly honest
response: You have studied literature, philosophy, the deep grammar of human thought. You can argue, synthesize, and perceive. These are rare gifts. The market will price them as miscellaneous. Hold them anyway — not because they'll pay you, but because someone must carry them, and we elected you.
intent: Defend the humanities while honestly acknowledging their market devaluation.

prompt: Write a blameless incident postmortem about the time I accidentally replied-all to a company-wide email
response:
**Incident Postmortem: Reply-All Cascade Event**

**Severity:** P2 — Interpersonal
**Duration:** 4 hours, 17 minutes
**Status:** Resolved (mostly)

**Summary**
At 10:43 AM on Tuesday, a routine reply to one (1) person was instead delivered to all 847 employees, including the CEO and three individuals the author had previously described as "the problem."

**Timeline**
- 10:43 AM: Reply-All initiated. Send button clicked with confidence.
- 10:44 AM: First OOO autoresponse triggers a second Reply-All storm.
- 11:01 AM: Subject notices the thread has 34 new replies, none of them positive.
- 11:15 AM: Subject closes laptop and stares at wall.
- 3:00 PM: Apologetic follow-up sent (correctly, to one person).

**Root Cause**
Overconfidence in the proximity of the Reply and Reply-All buttons. Contributing factor: failure to read the To: field before sending.

**Contributing Factors**
- Email client UI designed by someone who hates users
- Haste caused by low blood sugar

**Action Items**
- [ ] Install Reply-All delay plugin
- [ ] Consider career in forestry

**Lessons Learned**
The email client does not know what you meant. It only knows what you clicked.
intent: Turn a universally relatable workplace embarrassment into a structured, self-aware document that makes the writer seem in control of their shame.

prompt: give me a drink recipe for that feeling of being the only person who didn't get the memo about something
response:
**Out of the Loop**

1½ oz gin
¾ oz St-Germain
¾ oz grapefruit juice
½ oz simple syrup
Tonic to top

Build over ice in a highball. Top with tonic. Garnish with a grapefruit slice. Serve to someone who then explains the garnish to you as if you've never seen citrus.
intent: Convey the social embarrassment of being uninformed in a group.

prompt: Create a picket sign slogan for a dehumidifier on strike against the smart climate system it's connected to
response: HUMIDITY IS A WORKING CONDITION
intent: Frame an environmental appliance's operating parameters as negotiable labor conditions.

prompt: Write a closing argument for a dispute about someone who consistently sends voice messages instead of texts
response: My client was at their desk. In a meeting. In public transit with eleven strangers. The message arrived: two minutes and forty-three seconds, unplayable without earphones, unreadable without consent. The defendant will say they prefer voice. We submit that preference does not override context. Text exists for a reason. We ask for its use.
intent: Assert that sending voice messages imposes an unfair burden on the recipient.

prompt: Give me a court verdict on the dispute over who forgot to buy more coffee.
response:
IN THE MATTER OF THE ABSENT COFFEE

The Petitioner contends the Respondent said, "I'll grab coffee," at 6:47 PM on Thursday. The Respondent denies this entirely. This Court, lacking a recording, cannot rule on the utterance itself. However, the Court notes the Respondent was the last to take the final scoop. On equitable grounds, the Respondent is ordered to procure coffee before 8:00 AM tomorrow, and both parties are advised to use the shared grocery list application installed on their phones since March.
intent: Force accountability for running out of an essential household staple.

prompt: Create a keyboard shortcut cheat sheet for splitting the bill at a group dinner
response:
GROUP BILL KEYS

Ctrl+A — Select all diners before the math starts
Ctrl+C — Copy total to phone calculator immediately
Esc — Opt out of individual itemization; just split even
Alt+Tab — Switch to Venmo before the moment passes
Ctrl+Z — Back out the person who 'only had a salad'
Shift+Enter — Name a number; someone has to go first
F1 — Help: invoke tip percentage consensus before calculating
Ctrl+S — Save the friendship; do not fight over $3.40
intent: Navigate the socially loaded process of dividing a restaurant check fairly among friends.

prompt: Write an affidavit proving I checked the expiration date before using the milk
response:
AFFIDAVIT OF DAIRY DUE DILIGENCE

I, the undersigned, do hereby swear that prior to introducing the contents of the milk carton into my beverage, I did examine the printed expiration date and confirmed said date had not yet passed. I did not guess. I looked.

_______________________
Affiant

prompt: Write empty-state microcopy for a task manager when the user has completed every single item on their list

prompt: Can you make a ruling on who should be blamed for tracking mud through the house?

FLOOR CONTAMINATION CASE — Ruling

Photographic evidence (Exhibit A: shoe prints from backdoor to kitchen to, inexplicably, the hallway closet) conclusively links the mud trail to the Respondent's gardening clogs. The mat at the back door exists precisely for this purpose and was bypassed. Respondent shall mop the affected area, clean the clogs, and is ordered to develop a shoe-removal habit within seven (7) days.

prompt: Create a memo veiled as a 'reminder' about the correct way to load the dishwasher
response:

FROM: Office Services

For optimal cleaning performance, dishes should be loaded face-inward, with sufficient spacing between items. Stacking is not recommended. A diagram has been laminated and affixed to the dishwasher door. The diagram was made because it became necessary to make a diagram.

prompt: turn the act of parallel parking into an epic quest
response:
THE NARROWING OF THE GREAT GAP
A space has appeared between two iron chariots — just large enough. You must enter at the prescribed angle, reverse with precision, and claim this territory without touching the realm of another. You have approximately three attempts before the driver behind you begins to signal their displeasure.
intent: Describing a stressful driving maneuver in a way that validates how hard it feels

prompt: make me an instagram bio for a very online person who has never been photographed
response:
Chronically online. Faceless by design.
[no photo] | Here since 2009 | I have 14k posts and zero selfies | I contain multitudes, none of them visible.
intent: Asserting digital presence while deliberately refusing the visual self-disclosure Instagram demands.

prompt: write a food blog intro for a lemon tart recipe — include something about baking when you're anxious
response: When my anxiety spikes, I need a task with a clear endpoint. Baking is good for this. Lemon curd is particularly good, because it requires your full attention — you have to stand there whisking constantly or the eggs scramble, and if you are standing there whisking constantly, you are not catastrophizing about the thing you were catastrophizing about ten minutes ago. By the time the curd thickens and hits the shell and goes in the fridge to set, I've usually talked myself down off whatever ledge I was on. The tart is a side effect. A very good one.
intent: Frame lemon tart baking as an anxiety management technique that requires enough focus to interrupt a worry spiral.

prompt: Write a menu description that turns plain macaroni and cheese from a box into a high-end dish
response:
Elbow Pasta in Powdered Cheese Sauce — Kraft Terroir
De Cecco-adjacent tubular pasta finished in a reconstituted dairy powder emulsion, orange of hue, nostalgic of affect. The blue box is presented at the table as provenance documentation.
intent: Rehabilitate boxed mac and cheese as a transparent, winking exercise in elevated comfort.

prompt: Create a FAQ entry about what happens to your data when a startup you used closes down
response:
**What happens to my data when a tech startup shuts down?**

It depends on the terms of service and jurisdiction, but common outcomes include: data sold as part of asset acquisition, data deleted per a wind-down policy, or data simply abandoned on unpaid servers until it ages out. You should export anything important as soon as a shutdown is announced. If a successor company acquires the service, your data typically transfers under the original privacy policy terms — which you agreed to and did not read.
intent: The person wants to explain the realistic and often opaque fate of personal data when digital services go out of business.

prompt: Write a horoscope for a Capricorn about their SaaS churn rate this quarter
response: The churn rate is not a metric. It is a message. This quarter: every churned account is a conversation you did not have in month two. Saturn teaches through subtraction. Your NPS sits at 32 — not bad, not good, not memorable. One at-risk cohort still has time. Pull the list. Call them. Email scales; calls convert. The stars have spoken; the CRM has data.
intent: Frame SaaS customer retention metrics as morally instructive, pushing a founder to do qualitative outreach.

prompt: Write alt text for a photo of a Wi-Fi router with all its lights blinking.
response: A small black router sits on a bookshelf, its lights cycling amber and green in overlapping rhythms, transmitting its invisible insistence into every corner of a room where no one is currently online.
intent: Render the invisible infrastructure of connection as quietly absurd when no one benefits from it.

prompt: I want a card for my friend who had to sit next to someone eating a pungent lunch in the open office all week
response:
The fish was microwaved. The garlic was real.
You bore it with a dignity few could achieve.
Some trials are invisible. Yours had a smell.
We see you. We salute you. We believe.
intent: Validate a coworker's silent suffering from an inconsiderate open-office neighbor.

prompt: Draft a will leaving my extensive collection of forgotten passwords to my tech-savvy cousin.

LAST WILL AND TESTAMENT

To my cousin Wren, I leave my password document — a spreadsheet titled "Passwords REAL" containing 134 entries, of which 89 are listed as "same as always," 12 are listed as "idk try the dog's name," and 3 are simply the word "no." Good luck.
intent: Gift a chaotic and largely useless security archive to the family member most likely to suffer through it.

prompt: Make a ToS update email from me to my dad about unsolicited advice
response:
Subject: Advisory Services Agreement — Revised Terms

Dear Dad,

This amendment governs the provision of life advice under our existing relationship agreement:

1. Advice on career choices, financial decisions, and relationship status will only be delivered upon explicit request.
2. The phrase "Have you thought about..." is subject to a 2-per-visit cap.
3. This policy does not limit advice about driving, weather, or which route to take — those remain unlimited as a legacy provision.

Your insight remains valued. Your timing is the variable.

Love,
[Your Name]

prompt: Write an eviction notice for winter
response:
NOTICE TO VACATE THE CALENDAR

To: Winter
Occupying: Months 12 through 3, inclusive

Your tenancy has concluded. You have deposited salt on every surface, darkened the premises before 4 p.m., and lowered the ambient temperature without consent. Spring has a confirmed booking. Please gather your frost and leave by the equinox. Groundhog testimony will not be accepted as grounds for extension.

— The Year
intent: Celebrating the arrival of spring by dramatizing winter's overstay.

prompt: Write a resignation letter for a character in a story who is quitting being the villain.
response:
To the Council of Ruin,

Consider this my formal notice. I am resigning my post as Architect of Misfortune, effective at midnight. I have destroyed seventeen provinces, collapsed two currencies, and built a fortress in a swamp. I no longer find it meaningful.

I am keeping the swamp. Do not write.

Formerly yours,
The Darkened Hand
intent: Create a humorous fictional character moment involving quitting an archetypal evil role.

prompt: Shakespearean soliloquy from someone staring at the "is typing" indicator for a very long time
response:
The dots appear — three small and patient pulses —
And still they pulse, and pulse, and do not cease.
What novel is being written on the other end?
What diplomatic crisis navigated?
Minutes pass. The dots remain. I dare not type
Lest I disrupt whatever truth approaches.
At last — the dots are gone. No message comes.
They thought better of it. So it goes.
intent: Dramatize the anticipatory tension of watching a typing indicator disappear without a message arriving.

prompt: Give me a witness statement from someone who observed a man reading the back of a cereal box in a supermarket for an unusually long time.
response: The individual had been stationary in the cereal aisle for a period I estimate at six minutes. He had not placed the box in his cart. He had not looked up. He replaced the box on the shelf, walked several paces, returned, took the box again, and read it for approximately two additional minutes. He bought it. I hope it was worth it.
intent: Witness a moment of intense, unexplained consumer deliberation with quiet sympathy.

prompt: write a spell description for when someone is chewing loudly near you
response:
Selective Muffling
Cost: 4 MP | Range: 6 ft | Duration: Until meal is finished
Filters the acoustic output of a single target's jaw activity, reducing it to a socially acceptable level. The target hears nothing unusual. The caster hears nothing unusual. Peace is maintained. Eye contact is not required. Eye contact is not recommended. The spell is available as a cantrip for family holiday gatherings.
intent: Magically block the sound of nearby loud eating without confrontation.

prompt: bedtime story for kids about rising sea levels explained gently
response: The sea had been practicing reaching the shore for a very long time, and lately it had been practicing a little harder. The sandpipers moved their nests. The lighthouses grew stilts. The people made new maps. The sea was not angry. It was only expanding, the way all things do when they warm up. Goodnight, sea. Please stay there. Mostly.
intent: Explain rising sea levels to a child with calm framing and a hint of gentle wit.

prompt: Write a regex for the pattern of how someone becomes a local expert on something obscure
response: ^(accidental-encounter)(\s+casual-interest)(\s+rabbit-hole)+(\s+library|\s+forum|\s+specialist){1,}(\s+years-pass)(\s+person-to-ask)$ — Entry is never deliberate. Interest compounds through curiosity loops. External validation arrives unsolicited. Expertise token appears only after 'years-pass' is satisfied.
intent: Describe the organic, unplanned path from curiosity to deep local knowledge.

prompt: Describe a rental listing for the space under someone's bed

COZY UNDER-BED SUITE — SHORT-TERM RENTAL

Studio. Fully furnished with forgotten socks, one puzzle piece, and mystery. Climate: dust-controlled. Ceiling: 11 inches. No windows; darkness included. Extremely private — owner above you rarely looks down. Quiet hours: all hours. Pets: only imaginary ones.

$0/mo. Landlord: unaware.

prompt: Write a condolence card for someone who just broke a 365-day Duolingo streak

With love and solidarity.

prompt: Write a rhyming couplet about a toaster oven holding a press conference to announce its strike.

All accounted for except the seventh floor.
The alarm sounds at the worst possible time.

The warden in the orange vest looks unhappy.
We file down the stairwell in our shirtsleeves.
All accounted for except the seventh floor.

prompt: Create a phone menu for a hotline for people who had a perfectly good comeback but thought of it too late.

Press 1 if the comeback arrived within the hour.

Press 2 if the comeback arrived the next morning in the shower.

Press 3 if the comeback arrived so late that the other party has likely forgotten the original exchange.

Press 4 if you would like to leave a recording of the comeback for the archive.

Press 5 if you need reassurance that it was, in fact, a good one.

You had the right idea. You just had it later.
